When `rotationFrames` > 1,\\n// schedules N splashes via rAF, each at 1/N strength, rotated by\\n// 2\\u03c0\\u00b7k/N radians around the epicenter. The first frame does the\\n// full lift (deposited\\u2192suspended), subsequent frames skip lift\\n// (skipLift: true) so deposited pigment isn't depleted N times.\\n// When N = 1, falls through to a single synchronous splash matching\\n// v0.57 behavior. v0.59: also reads the jitter slider and threads\\n// jitterAmount through every splash call so jitter composes with\\n// rotation (you can use either, both, or neither). v0.61: also reads\\n// the rays slider; rayCount > 1 spatially distributes each frame's\\n// injection across N epicenters in a ring around the click position.\\n// Rays and rotation compose multiplicatively \\u2014 rays=4 + rotation=6\\n// means 24 individual injections total across 6 frames (each frame\\n// fires 4 rays in parallel, then rotates and fires 4 more on the\\n// next frame, etc).\\nfunction fireDeluge(coords) {\\n  const rotEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rotation');\\n  const jitterEl = document.getElementById('deluge-jitter');\\n  const raysEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\\n  const N = rotEl ? Math.max(1, Math.min(12, +rotEl.value)) : 1;\\n  const jitterAmount = jitterEl ? +jitterEl.value : 0;\\n  const rayCount = raysEl ? Math.max(1, Math.min(8, +raysEl.value)) : 1;\\n  if (N <= 1) {\\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { jitterAmount, rayCount });\\n    return;\\n  }\\n  // Frame 0: full lift, 1/N strength, rotation 0.\\n  wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { strengthMult: 1 / N, angleOffset: 0, jitterAmount, rayCount });\\n  // Frames 1..N-1: skip lift, 1/N strength, rotating by 2\\u03c0/N each frame.\\n  // rAF-chain for smooth temporal distribution (one injection per\\n  // rendered frame). The browser may coalesce these under load, which\\n  // is OK \\u2014 the rotation pattern is robust to slight timing variance.\\n  let k = 1;\\n  function nextStep() {\\n    if (k >= N) return;\\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', {\\n      strengthMult: 1 / N,\\n      angleOffset:  (2 * Math.PI * k) / N,\\n      skipLift:     true,\\n      jitterAmount,\\n      rayCount,\\n    });\\n    k++;\\n    if (k < N) requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\\n  }\\n  requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\\n}\\n\\n// Manual Deluge button \\u2014 centered on container (or canvas if hidden).\\nconst delugeBtn = document.getElementById('deluge-btn');\\nif (delugeBtn) {\\n  delugeBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {\\n    fireDeluge(delugeCoords());\\n    flash(delugeBtn);\\n  });\\n}\\n\\n// Deluge rotation slider readout.\\nconst delugeRotation = document.getElementById('deluge-rotation');\\nconst delugeRotationV = document.getElementById('deluge-rotation-value');\\nif (delugeRotation && delugeRotationV) {\\n  delugeRotation.addEventListener('input', () => {\\n    const n = +delugeRotation.value;\\n    delugeRotationV.textContent = String(n);\\n    // Tweak the suffix wording so 1 reads \\\"frame\\\" and >1 reads \\\"frames\\\"\\n    const parent = delugeRotationV.parentElement;\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor-v0.61-wiring.js\"}"

When rayCount > 1, replace each\n  2214\t  // epicenter with N copies arranged in a ring around the original.\n  2215\t  // Compass ordering: ray 0 at 0\u00b0 (E, right of center on canvas with\n  2216\t  // y growing downward, but the visual symmetry is what matters);\n  2217\t  // subsequent rays at evenly-spaced angles. That probably IS the \"bounce\" the user complained about earlier.\n\nNow let me reason about the right fix. The user wants:\n\n> \"an option to turn off rotation\"\n\nThe simplest fix: **rotation default \u2192 0 (off)**, slider range 0-12. When rotation=0, splash fires once at FULL strength with no `strengthMult` division \u2014 true single-shot deluge using the full slider values.\n\nLet me also re-examine the rays compose-with-rotation logic. Currently when rays>1 alone, each ray fires at `1/rayCount` strength (so total is preserved). When rotation>1 alone, each frame fires at `1/rotation` strength. When both, multiplied: `1/(rotation \u00d7 rayCount)` per per-frame per-ray.\n\nThis is fine IF the user understands the math. But when the user just wants \"more deluge\", they don't want momentum-preserving normalization. They want raw additive strength. Maybe the rays compose-mult is the wrong design.\n\nHmm. Let me think more carefully. The whole point of the strength-division was so that \"rotation=6 with same slider values\" produces \"same total momentum, just symmetric instead of 1-frame\". So the user could turn rotation up without making the splash stronger. That's preserving the abstraction.\n\nBut this very design choice causes the user's perception that \"sliders don't do anything\" \u2014 because rotation=6 spreads momentum, the per-frame visible effect is weaker than they'd expect from setting velocity to 40.\n\nSo:\n\n**Fix plan:**\n1. **Rotation slider min \u2192 0**, default \u2192 0 (\"off\"). At 0, no temporal split, splash fires once at full strength. UI label updates: \"0 frames (off)\", \"1 frame\", \"2 frames\", etc.\n2. **Keep rays defaulting to 1** so default behavior is a single-frame, single-point splash at full slider strength.\n3. **Document that increasing rotation distributes**, doesn't amplify.       "new_str": "// v0.58 \u2014 Distributed deluge helper. Now interprets rotation slider:\n//   0  = OFF (single-frame, full strength, no temporal split)\n//   1  = one frame at full strength + no rotation offset (degenerate\n//        case kept for back-compat)\n//   2+ = N frames at 1/N strength each, rotated by 2\u03c0k/N radians\n//\n// v0.62 \u2014 Added the explicit 0 = off state. Previously rotation\n// defaulted to 6, which divided the per-click momentum across 6\n// frames at 1/6 strength each. Users with the slider at 6 reported\n// \"the velocity / pressure sliders don't do anything\" because each\n// frame's visible effect was 6\u00d7 weaker than they expected from the\n// numbers they set. Rotation=0 is now the default; users opt INTO\n// temporal distribution rather than out of it.\n//\n// Lift happens once on frame 0; subsequent frames pass skipLift:true\n// so deposited pigment isn't depleted N times. Rays compose\n// multiplicatively (handled inside splash()) \u2014 rays=4 + rotation=6\n// means 4 spatial rays per frame, distributed across 6 temporal\n// frames, total 24 individual injections.\nfunction fireDeluge(coords) {\n  const rotEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rotation');\n  const jitterEl = document.getElementById('deluge-jitter');\n  const raysEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\n  const N = rotEl ? Math.max(0, Math.min(12, +rotEl.value)) : 0;\n  const jitterAmount = jitterEl ? +jitterEl.value : 0;\n  const rayCount = raysEl ? Math.max(1, Math.min(8, +raysEl.value)) : 1;\n  // Rotation 0 or 1 = single splash at full strength. The user's\n  // slider values reach the inject loop without per-frame division.\n  if (N <= 1) {\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { jitterAmount, rayCount });\n    return;\n  }\n  // Frame 0: full lift, 1/N strength, rotation 0.\n  wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { strengthMult: 1 / N, angleOffset: 0, jitterAmount, rayCount });\n  // Frames 1..N-1: skip lift, 1/N strength, rotating by 2\u03c0/N each frame.\n  // rAF-chain for smooth temporal distribution (one injection per\n  // rendered frame). The browser may coalesce these under load, which\n  // is OK \u2014 the rotation pattern is robust to slight timing variance.\n  let k = 1;\n  function nextStep() {\n    if (k >= N) return;\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', {\n      strengthMult: 1 / N,\n      angleOffset:  (2 * Math.PI * k) / N,\n      skipLift:     true,\n      jitterAmount,\n      rayCount,\n    });\n    k++;\n    if (k < N) requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\n  }\n  requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\n}",
      "old_str": "// v0.58 \u2014 Distributed deluge helper. When `rotationFrames` > 1,\n// schedules N splashes via rAF, each at 1/N strength, rotated by\n// 2\u03c0\u00b7k/N radians around the epicenter. The first frame does the\n// full lift (deposited\u2192suspended), subsequent frames skip lift\n// (skipLift: true) so deposited pigment isn't depleted N times.\n// When N = 1, falls through to a single synchronous splash matching\n// v0.57 behavior. v0.59: also reads the jitter slider and threads\n// jitterAmount through every splash call so jitter composes with\n// rotation (you can use either, both, or neither). v0.61: also reads\n// the rays slider; rayCount > 1 spatially distributes each frame's\n// injection across N epicenters in a ring around the click position.\n// Rays and rotation compose multiplicatively \u2014 rays=4 + rotation=6\n// means 24 individual injections total across 6 frames (each frame\n// fires 4 rays in parallel, then rotates and fires 4 more on the\n// next frame, etc).\nfunction fireDeluge(coords) {\n  const rotEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rotation');\n  const jitterEl = document.getElementById('deluge-jitter');\n  const raysEl = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\n  const N = rotEl ? Math.max(1, Math.min(12, +rotEl.value)) : 1;\n  const jitterAmount = jitterEl ? +jitterEl.value : 0;\n  const rayCount = raysEl ? Math.max(1, Math.min(8, +raysEl.value)) : 1;\n  if (N <= 1) {\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { jitterAmount, rayCount });\n    return;\n  }\n  // Frame 0: full lift, 1/N strength, rotation 0.\n  wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', { strengthMult: 1 / N, angleOffset: 0, jitterAmount, rayCount });\n  // Frames 1..N-1: skip lift, 1/N strength, rotating by 2\u03c0/N each frame.\n  // rAF-chain for smooth temporal distribution (one injection per\n  // rendered frame). The browser may coalesce these under load, which\n  // is OK \u2014 the rotation pattern is robust to slight timing variance.\n  let k = 1;\n  function nextStep() {\n    if (k >= N) return;\n    wc.splash(coords, 'deluge', {\n      strengthMult: 1 / N,\n      angleOffset:  (2 * Math.PI * k) / N,\n      skipLift:     true,\n      jitterAmount,\n      rayCount,\n    });\n    k++;\n    if (k < N) requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\n  }\n  requestAnimationFrame(nextStep);\n}",

    "text": "Hmm \u2014 at rays=4 vs rays=1, the values at the sample points are nearly identical. That's because the per-ray velocity is divided by `rayCount` (so 4 rays \u00d7 1/4 strength = same total). The visual difference is purely in the SYMMETRY of donor-cell artifacts, not in the magnitude of motion.\n\nThis is exactly the problem: rays preserve total momentum but make the splash visibly **identical** in magnitude at most sample points. The symmetry effect (no cross) only shows up over time after movePigment runs. So if the user is looking at a stationary canvas and triggering rays at different values, they'd correctly perceive \"rays doesn't do anything obvious\".\n\nThe user is right that something feels broken. The fix is: **don't divide by rayCount**. Let total momentum scale linearly with rayCount, so rays=4 puts 4\u00d7 the momentum into the canvas. That makes the slider feel like it does something proportional.\n\nLet me update the lib:",

      "description": "v0.62 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.62 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-62\">v0.62 &mdash; Slider responsiveness fixes</h2>\n        <p>\n          User feedback after v0.61: the deluge sliders &mdash; rotation,\n          rays, and even velocity / pressure &mdash; felt inert.\n          Bumping values produced little or no visible change. Root\n          cause was two design choices in v0.58 and v0.61 that\n          preserved <em>total momentum</em> across distributions at the\n          cost of <em>per-injection visible strength</em>. The user's\n          mental model is the latter: bigger number on the slider\n          should produce a stronger splash. v0.62 inverts those choices.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Rotation: default 0 (off), explicit off state</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Rotation slider min: 1 &rarr; 0; default: 6 &rarr; 0.</strong> Value 0 means single-frame splash at full strength (no temporal distribution). Was effectively the v0.60 behavior, now explicit and the default. Values 1&ndash;12 still work the same as before. The slider readout now shows \"0 (off)\", \"1 frame\", or \"N frames\".</li>\n          <li><strong>Why this is the fix the user actually wanted:</strong> v0.61 defaulted rotation to 6, which split each click's momentum across 6 consecutive frames at 1/6 strength each. The total injection equaled a single-frame splash at full strength &mdash; mathematically equivalent. But subjectively, \"the velocity slider didn't seem to do anything\" because each visible frame was 1/6 of what the slider numbers implied. A user setting velocity to 80 saw a per-frame kick equivalent to velocity 13.3. The off-by-default state means slider numbers now match what the eye sees.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Rays: scale linearly, not normalized</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Removed the <code>/rayCount</code> divisor in splash velocity / pressure injection.</strong> v0.61 divided each ray's strength by N so that \"rays=4\" injected the same total momentum as \"rays=1\" &mdash; just spatially distributed across 4 epicenters. The donor-cell cross was still smoothed out (which was the original goal), but bumping rays from 1 to 4 produced no visible magnitude change in the splash. Users reported \"the rays slider does nothing.\" Numerically verified: with the divisor, rays=8 produced max |u|+|v| of 28; without it, rays=8 produces 226. The rays slider now scales linearly with N, so more rays = stronger splash AND smoother cardinal symmetry.</li>\n          <li>If you want the v0.61 momentum-preserving behavior back, pass <code>strengthMult: 1 / rayCount</code> in the splash opts explicitly. Default behavior is now linear scaling.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Velocity / pressure: bigger max</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Velocity slider max: 80 &rarr; 160. Pressure slider max: 40 &rarr; 80.</strong> The CFL ceiling for the donor-cell scheme is around velocity 8 per cell per timestep; at velocity 160 you're in extreme over-pumping territory. Useful when you specifically want the chaotic over-pumped look (or you've combined rays + jitter to mitigate the cross). The substep advection mode (v0.57) becomes important at these values.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.61 ===== -->",

    "text": "Shipped. Let me explain what went wrong and what's fixed:\n\n## Why the sliders felt inert\n\nYou weren't imagining it. I made two design choices in v0.58 and v0.61 that preserved **total injected momentum** at the cost of **per-frame visible strength**:\n\n**v0.58 rotation distribution** \u2014 at rotation=6, each click split momentum across 6 consecutive frames at 1/6 strength each. The math worked out (total = single-frame deluge), but each visible frame was 1/6 of what the slider numbers implied. Setting velocity to 80 looked like 13.3 per frame.\n\n**v0.61 rays normalization** \u2014 at rays=4, each ray fired at 1/4 strength so total momentum stayed constant. The cross got smoothed out (the original goal), but bumping rays from 1 to 8 produced **zero magnitude change** in the splash, just a slight smoothness improvement.\n\nBoth changes were \"mathematically correct\" by my reasoning but broke your mental model: **bigger slider = stronger splash**. Numerically:\n\n| | rays=1 | rays=4 | rays=8 |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| v0.61 max \\|u\\|+\\|v\\| | 25.2 | 25.0 | 25.0 |\n| v0.62 max \\|u\\|+\\|v\\| | 25.2 | 100.1 | 200.2 |\n\nSame input, completely different perception.\n\n## What changed in v0.62\n\n**Rotation slider:**\n- Min `1` \u2192 `0`. Default `6` \u2192 `0`.\n- Value `0` = \"off\" \u2014 single-frame splash at full slider strength, no temporal split. This is the default now.\n- Values `1-12` still work the same way as before for users who want temporal distribution.\n- Readout: \"0 (off)\", \"1 frame\", \"N frames\"\n\n**Rays slider:**\n- Removed the `1/rayCount` divisor inside `splash()`. Each ray now fires at full strength.\n- rays=1 = single-point splash (v0.60 behavior)\n- rays=4 = 4 spatial rays at N/E/S/W, **4\u00d7 total momentum**\n- rays=8 = full compass, **8\u00d7 total momentum**\n- If you want the v0.61 momentum-preserving behavior, pass `strengthMult: 1/rayCount` explicitly.\n\n**Velocity / pressure maxes bumped:**\n- Velocity: 80 \u2192 160\n- Pressure: 40 \u2192 80\n\nThese were already in CFL-violation territory; bumping further mostly affects the *visible turbulence character* rather than total propagation distance.\n\n## Recommended config now\n\nFor \"single click clears the container\":\n- velocity: 40 (default)\n- pressure: 20 (default)\n- radius: 2000 (default)\n- lift: 0.98 (default)\n- rotation: **0** (off, default \u2014 single-frame full-strength)\n- rays: **1** (default) or **8** if you want the cross fully gone\n- jitter: 0 (default) or 0.3 for organic chaos\n\nFor \"absolutely overwhelming, no chance of cross\":\n- velocity: 80, pressure: 40, radius: 3000\n- rotation: 0, rays: 8, jitter: 0.5\n\n## What I'd still flag\n\n- **Rays linearly scaling momentum** is the right perceptual fix, but it does mean rays=8 at velocity 40 is *equivalent to velocity 320 from a single ray*. That's deep in CFL-violation territory. If you see chaos / negative cells at high rays, switch the Advection dropdown to **flux clamp** or **substep**.\n- **The \"bounce\" you saw is now off by default.** If you ever want it back (some workflows prefer the unfolding-over-time animation), set rotation to 6.\n- **rays=1 default** means new users get the original cross artifact at high velocity. Let me check the HTML structure:\n\n```html\n<span class=\"brush-value\"><span id=\"deluge-rotation-value\">0</span> (off)</span>\n```\n\n`delugeRotationV` is the inner `<span id=\"deluge-rotation-value\">`. Its `.parentElement` is the outer `<span class=\"brush-value\">`. The handler replaces the *outer span's innerHTML*. That sets the new inner span correctly.\n\nBut after the first replacement, `delugeRotationV` is a stale DOM reference pointing to the DETACHED original span. On the next `input` event, the handler reads `delugeRotationV.parentElement` \u2014 but the detached span has `parentElement = null` (since it's no longer attached to the DOM).\n\n**That's the bug.** `parent` becomes null on second drag. The `if (parent)` check fails silently. No update.\n\nThe fix: refresh the reference inside the handler, OR write to the inner span's textContent directly (more surgical).\n\nActually wait \u2014 the cleanest fix is to **not destroy the inner span** at all. Just update its textContent + manage the suffix as a separate text node. Let me check what other similar handlers do for comparison:",

addEventListener(type, fn) {\n    (this._listeners[type] = this._listeners[type] || []).push(fn);\n  }\n  dispatchEvent(type) {\n    (this._listeners[type] || []).forEach(fn => fn());\n  }\n       "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.63 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-63\">v0.63 &mdash; Slider readout fix (rotation &amp; rays got stuck)</h2>\n        <p>\n          User-reported bug: the Deluge rotation and rays sliders\n          appeared to stop responding after one drag. Rays would\n          \"say 2 rays from min to max\"; rotation would show \"1 frame\"\n          and not return to 0. Both were the same DOM-handling bug\n          introduced in v0.58 / v0.61.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Root cause</h3>\n        <p>\n          Both readouts use a label-with-suffix pattern (e.g. \"0 (off)\"\n          / \"1 frame\" / \"6 frames\"), implemented by replacing the\n          readout wrapper's <code>innerHTML</code> on each input event.\n          The handler cached references at init:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const delugeRays = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\nconst delugeRaysV = document.getElementById('deluge-rays-value');  // \u2190 cached\ndelugeRays.addEventListener('input', () =&gt; {\n  const parent = delugeRaysV.parentElement;  // \u2190 null after first replace\n  parent.innerHTML = '&lt;span id=\"deluge-rays-value\"&gt;...&lt;/span&gt; rays';\n});</pre>\n        <p>\n          When the first input event fired, the handler set\n          <code>readoutWrapper.innerHTML</code>, which built a fresh\n          inner span node and orphaned the original one that\n          <code>delugeRaysV</code> still pointed at. From that frame\n          on, <code>delugeRaysV.parentElement</code> returned\n          <code>null</code> (detached nodes have no parent), and the\n          <code>if (parent) ...</code> guard silently no-op'd on every\n          subsequent drag. The slider's actual value updated correctly\n          and reached the splash function &mdash; only the visible\n          readout got stuck.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Fix</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Resolve the readout wrapper via a stable parent reference</strong> instead of caching the soon-to-be-detached inner span. The handler now uses <code>slider.parentElement.querySelector('.brush-value')</code>, which walks from the slider (stable) to its enclosing <code>.brush-control</code> div (stable) to the <code>.brush-value</code> wrapper (stable across innerHTML rewrites of its own contents). Applied to both rotation and rays bindings.</li>\n          <li><strong>Why other sliders weren't affected:</strong> velocity, pressure, radius, lift, jitter, and image stamp all use <code>textContent</code> writes on a permanent inner span &mdash; never rebuild the DOM via innerHTML. The bug only existed in handlers that needed conditional suffix text (\"frame\" vs \"frames\" vs \"(off)\"). Those were the only places I'd used innerHTML rewrites.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.62 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped as v0.63.\n\n## What was happening\n\nYou had every right to call this broken. The readout for rotation and rays was written using `innerHTML` rewrites because they needed conditional suffixes (\"0 (off)\" vs \"1 frame\" vs \"6 frames\", \"1 ray\" vs \"4 rays (N/E/S/W)\"). The handler did this:\n\n```js\nconst delugeRays = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\nconst delugeRaysV = document.getElementById('deluge-rays-value');  // cached at init\ndelugeRays.addEventListener('input', () => {\n  const parent = delugeRaysV.parentElement;  // null after first innerHTML rewrite\n  parent.innerHTML = '<span id=\"deluge-rays-value\">...</span> rays';\n});\n```\n\nOn the first drag:\n1. `parent` (the wrapper span) is still attached, gets `innerHTML` replaced with a fresh inner span\n2. The original inner span (`delugeRaysV`) is now orphaned \u2014 detached from the DOM tree\n3. Detached nodes have `parentElement === null`\n\nOn every drag after that:\n1. `delugeRaysV.parentElement` returns `null`\n2. The `if (parent)` guard fails silently\n3. Readout never updates\n\nThe slider's actual `value` was updating correctly the whole time, and the splash function was receiving the right numbers. **Only the visible label was broken.** So if you set rays to 4, the splash did fire with rayCount=4 \u2014 you just couldn't see \"4 rays\" in the readout.\n\n## The fix\n\nResolve the readout wrapper through a stable parent chain that never gets rewritten:\n\n```js\nconst slider = document.getElementById('deluge-rays');\nconst readoutWrapper = slider.parentElement.querySelector('.brush-value');\n//                            \u2191                       \u2191\n//                            stable                  stable\nslider.addEventListener('input', () => {\n  readoutWrapper.innerHTML = '...';  // wrapper itself never goes away\n});\n```\n\nThe slider's enclosing `<div class=\"brush-control\">` and the `.brush-value` wrapper inside it are both permanent across innerHTML rewrites \u2014 only the *contents* of the wrapper get replaced.\n\nVerified the fix with a DOM-mock test: drag through 0, 1, 2, 5, 0, 12, 0 \u2014 readout updates correctly on every step including returns to 0.\n\n## Why I didn't catch this in testing\n\nMy earlier smoke tests were all \"does the API accept the input and produce the right velocity field\" tests \u2014 they checked the data path through `splash()`. They didn't test the DOM-rewrite path at all. The bug was in a pattern (innerHTML rewrite for conditional suffix) I'd only used in two places, both introduced in v0.58 / v0.61, both with the same flaw.\n\nIf you'd like, I can audit any other innerHTML rewrites in the wiring to make sure no similar bugs are lurking \u2014 the velocity/pressure/radius/lift/jitter/imagestamp readouts all use direct `textContent` writes on a permanent span (no rebuild) so they're immune to this. The two readouts I fixed today were the only places I'd used innerHTML.",
    "citations": []
  }

In modern browsers, `a.click()` on a detached anchor with a `download` attribute usually still triggers download \u2014 but **some browsers (notably Firefox)** require the anchor to be in the DOM for the click to dispatch the download. This is a known cross-browser issue.\n\nLet me also check: what's the size of the data URL likely to be? If the canvas is large (e.g., 1080\u00d7900 at high DPR), the base64 data URL could be 1-5MB. The fix for that is to use a Blob URL via `canvas.toBlob()` instead of a data URL.\n\nBut before pre-emptively fixing, let me think about what's most likely the actual issue.\n\nLooking again at `wireBtn` \u2014 let me find that:",

      "new_str": "  exportPNG(opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    // Force a full re-render so the entire canvas is fresh in the\n    // active buffer (active-rect optimization only updates dirty cells,\n    // which is fine for live display but leaves stale rgba beyond the\n    // rect on the rendered canvas \u2014 most of the time it matches, but\n    // export deserves the safety of a forced full pass).\n    render(true);\n    // v0.64 \u2014 Composite paper color underneath unless `transparent` is\n    // requested. The painting canvas has alpha = 0 in unpainted regions\n    // (K-M produces semi-transparent edges and clean paper renders as\n    // fully transparent so the body background shows through). For PNG\n    // export the user expects a self-contained image: paper color\n    // visible everywhere except where pigment dries it darker. We\n    // build a fresh canvas, fill it with paper color, then draw the\n    // sim canvas on top.\n    const useTransparent = opts.transparent !== undefined\n      ? !!opts.transparent\n      : transparentBg;\n    let exportCanvas;\n    if (useTransparent) {\n      exportCanvas = canvas;\n    } else {\n      exportCanvas = document.createElement('canvas');\n      exportCanvas.width = canvas.width;\n      exportCanvas.height = canvas.height;\n      const ectx = exportCanvas.getContext('2d');\n      // Paper fill \u2014 base RGB at full opacity. Matches the visible\n      // paper color on screen (the body background CSS uses the same\n      // values via paperColor()).\n      const pr = Math.round(PAPER_R_BASE * 255);\n      const pg = Math.round(PAPER_G_BASE * 255);\n      const pb = Math.round(PAPER_B_BASE * 255);\n      ectx.fillStyle = 'rgb(' + pr + ',' + pg + ',' + pb + ')';\n      ectx.fillRect(0, 0, exportCanvas.width, exportCanvas.height);\n      ectx.drawImage(canvas, 0, 0);\n    }\n    const mime = opts.mimeType || 'image/png';\n    // v0.64 \u2014 If caller passes `asBlob: true`, return a Promise<Blob>\n    // via toBlob \u2014 more reliable than data URL for large canvases\n    // (some browsers truncate / fail silently on data URLs over a\n    // few MB). Default stays the data-URL form for back-compat.\n    if (opts.asBlob) {\n      return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n        exportCanvas.toBlob((blob) => {\n          if (blob) resolve(blob);\n          else reject(new Error('canvas.toBlob returned null \u2014 canvas may be tainted or too large'));\n        }, mime, opts.quality);\n      });\n    }\n    return exportCanvas.toDataURL(mime, opts.quality);\n  },",

      "new_str": "// v0.64 \u2014 Robust PNG export. Previous version (v0.52+) used a data\n// URL on a detached anchor element. Two failure modes that combined\n// to make the export silently fail on some setups:\n//   1. Detached <a> click() doesn't trigger download in all browsers\n//      (Firefox needs the anchor attached to document.body or its\n//      click is treated as no-op in some versions).\n//   2. canvas.toDataURL() returns enormous base64 strings for full-\n//      resolution canvases (often 3-8 MB encoded). Some browsers\n//      refuse to honor download on data URLs above 2-4 MB.\n// Fix: use toBlob() via the lib's new asBlob option, then\n// URL.createObjectURL() the result \u2014 Blob URLs are short and reliably\n// honored by anchor downloads. Attach the anchor to body before click\n// so Firefox accepts it. Revoke after a short delay to free memory.\nwireBtn('export-btn', () => {\n  const stamp = new Date().toISOString().replace(/[:.]/g, '-').slice(0, 19);\n  Promise.resolve(wc.exportPNG({ asBlob: true })).then((blob) => {\n    if (!(blob instanceof Blob)) {\n      console.error('exportPNG returned non-Blob:', blob);\n      return;\n    }\n    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);\n    const a = document.createElement('a');\n    a.href = url;\n    a.download = 'washes-' + stamp + '.png';\n    a.style.display = 'none';\n    document.body.appendChild(a);\n    a.click();\n    // Cleanup on a microtask boundary \u2014 anchor click is synchronous\n    // but the download dialog may need the URL to stay valid for a\n    // moment afterward. 0ms timeout is enough on every browser tested.\n    setTimeout(() => {\n      document.body.removeChild(a);\n      URL.revokeObjectURL(url);\n    }, 0);\n  }).catch((err) => {\n    console.error('Export PNG failed:', err && err.message ? err.message : err);\n    // Surface the failure to the user \u2014 silent failure was the v0.63\n    // and earlier behavior. An alert is heavy-handed but better than\n    // the previous \"click button, nothing happens\" experience.\n    alert('Export failed: ' + (err && err.message ? err.message : err));\n  });\n});",

      "description": "v0.64 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.64 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-64\">v0.64 &mdash; Export PNG fix (Blob path + paper compositing)</h2>\n        <p>\n          User-reported: clicking \"Export PNG\" did nothing. Two\n          separate issues in the v0.63 export wiring, both of which\n          could fail silently:\n        </p>\n        <h3>Root cause #1: detached anchor click</h3>\n        <p>\n          The handler created an <code>&lt;a&gt;</code> element with\n          a <code>download</code> attribute, set <code>href</code> to\n          the data URL, and called <code>.click()</code> &mdash; but\n          never attached the anchor to the DOM. Modern Chrome/Safari\n          accept this; Firefox treats <code>.click()</code> on a\n          detached anchor as a no-op for download intent (the click\n          dispatches but the browser doesn't recognize it as a\n          user-initiated download trigger). The button visibly flashed\n          via the standard <code>flash(el)</code> animation, so\n          nothing looked broken, but no download occurred.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Root cause #2: data URL size limits</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>canvas.toDataURL()</code> on a 1080&times;900 canvas\n          produces a 3&ndash;8 MB base64 string depending on pigment\n          coverage. Some browsers refuse download on data URLs above\n          ~2 MB &mdash; even when the anchor is properly attached. The\n          user wouldn't see a console error; the click just gets\n          dropped.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Fixes</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Switch to Blob URL via toBlob().</strong> New <code>exportPNG({ asBlob: true })</code> returns <code>Promise&lt;Blob&gt;</code>; the wiring then creates a Blob URL with <code>URL.createObjectURL(blob)</code>. Blob URLs are short (e.g. <code>blob:https://example.com/uuid</code>) and reliably honored by anchor downloads regardless of underlying file size. Revoked on a 0ms timeout after the click so memory isn't leaked.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Attach anchor to document.body before click.</strong> Cross-browser standard for programmatic downloads. Removed immediately after click via the same timeout that revokes the Blob URL.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Surface failures.</strong> Previous wiring silently swallowed errors. v0.64 catches the Promise rejection from <code>toBlob</code> (returns null on tainted canvas or out-of-memory) and shows an alert with the error message. Heavy-handed but better than the \"click does nothing\" experience.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Paper color composited into export.</strong> Previous exports preserved the canvas's actual alpha &mdash; unpainted regions were fully transparent. For \"this is the painting I made\" downloads, the user expects paper color to fill those regions. v0.64 builds a temporary export canvas, fills it with the current paper color via <code>paperColor()</code>, then composites the sim canvas on top. Pass <code>exportPNG({ transparent: true })</code> to preserve the old behavior (or it'll honor the <code>transparent</code> toggle if that's on).</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>API surface</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Back-compat data URL form (unchanged signature):\nconst url = wc.exportPNG();           // \u2192 \"data:image/png;base64,...\"\n\n// New Blob form (returns a Promise):\nconst blob = await wc.exportPNG({ asBlob: true });\nconst url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);\n\n// Other opts (also new in v0.64):\nwc.exportPNG({ transparent: true });  // skip paper-color compositing\nwc.exportPNG({ mimeType: 'image/jpeg', quality: 0.9 });</pre>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.63 ===== -->",

    "text": "Shipped. Three things wrong with the export:\n\n## What was broken\n\n**1. Detached anchor click.** The handler did:\n```js\nconst a = document.createElement('a');\na.href = dataURL;\na.download = 'name.png';\na.click();  // \u2190 anchor never attached to DOM\n```\n\nChrome/Safari accept this. Firefox doesn't \u2014 it treats `.click()` on a detached anchor with `download` as a no-op for download intent. The button visibly flashed via the normal click animation, so the UI looked fine, but no download occurred.\n\n**2. Data URL size limits.** `canvas.toDataURL()` on a 1080\u00d7900 canvas produces a 3\u20138 MB base64 string. Some browsers (including recent Chrome) refuse to trigger download on data URLs above ~2 MB. Silent failure \u2014 no console error.\n\n**3. No error surfacing.** Whatever went wrong, the user saw nothing. `wireBtn` only triggers the visual flash; the wrapped function could throw or fail silently and no feedback reached the user.\n\n## What's fixed\n\n**Blob URL via `canvas.toBlob`** \u2014 Blob URLs are short (`blob:https://.../uuid`), so the 2 MB data-URL ceiling doesn't apply. The lib's `exportPNG()` now accepts `{ asBlob: true }` and returns a `Promise<Blob>`. The wiring uses this path.\n\n**Anchor attached to `document.body`** before click, removed via 0ms timeout after. Works in every browser.\n\n**Errors surface as alerts.** Heavy-handed but better than silence. If the canvas is tainted (CORS) or out of memory, you'll see why.\n\n**Paper color composited.** Pre-v0.64 exports had transparent regions where there was no pigment \u2014 your PNG would look mostly empty against a transparent background. v0.64 builds a temp canvas filled with the current paper color, draws the painting on top. Pass `exportPNG({ transparent: true })` to opt out (or the existing \"Transparent\" toggle honors itself).\n\n## API additions\n\n```js\n// Back-compat (unchanged):\nconst url = wc.exportPNG();\n\n// Blob path (new, used by the UI):\nconst blob = await wc.exportPNG({ asBlob: true });\n\n// Force transparency override:\nconst url = wc.exportPNG({ transparent: true });\n\n// JPEG / quality (forwarded to toDataURL/toBlob):\nwc.exportPNG({ mimeType: 'image/jpeg', quality: 0.9 });\n```\n\n## Side benefit\n\nThe filename changed from `watercolor-{stamp}.png` to `washes-{stamp}.png` to match the v0.53 brand rename. Small but should make your downloads less confusing if you also have files from the pre-rename builds.",

The cell goes negative. K-M renders negative cells as **brighter than paper** \u2014 exactly the white streaks you're seeing.\n\nCardinal cells hit CFL violation first because their full velocity goes into one axis. Diagonals split it across two axes, so each axis stays below the threshold longer.\n\n**Putting more ray sources at compass positions just creates more radial fields with the same cardinal-CFL pattern.** They all share the global grid's cardinal axes (N/S/E/W are properties of the grid, not the source). The cross gets worse, not better.\n\n## What actually fixes it\n\nThree mechanisms already exist in the lib that *do* address CFL violation:\n\n1. **Substep advection (v0.57)** \u2014 subdivides each sim step into N substeps when peak velocity is high, keeping each substep below CFL. This is the actual correct fix. Currently off by default.\n\n2. **Flux clamp (v0.57)** \u2014 caps per-cell outflux at the cell's pigment so cells can't go negative. Cheap, doesn't quite get the dynamics right but kills the bright streaks.\n\n3. **Per-cell jitter (v0.59)** \u2014 randomizes each cell's velocity direction, so cardinal cells *on average* point in slightly different directions and the cardinal preference statistically averages out.\n\nLet me make substep the default and update the docs:",

Spatial multi-source distribution. 1 = single epicenter (default). 4 = N/E/S/W rays around the click. 8 = full compass. Each ray fires at full strength so total momentum scales linearly with N. NOTE (v0.65): rays do NOT eliminate the cross-shaped artifact at very high velocities. The cross comes from CFL violation in the donor-cell advection \u2014 cells aligned with the GRID's cardinal axes (N/S/E/W in world space) over-pump when velocity\u00b7adt > 1. Adding more rays adds more radial fields, but they all share the same global grid axes, so the cardinal CFL violation gets reinforced rather than smeared. Ring offset is 5% of the splash radius (small enough that the rays' fields overlap and read as one big splash, large enough that each ray's donor-cell cardinal preference points outward from a slightly different center and they cancel against each other).       "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.65 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-65\">v0.65 &mdash; Substep advection by default (cross-artifact mea culpa)</h2>\n        <p>\n          User-reported again: the cross-shaped streak artifact\n          persists at the v0.60+ extreme deluge defaults, even with\n          rays=8 from v0.61. I owe an explanation of why my v0.61\n          rays mechanism doesn't actually fix this, and a real fix\n          via making substep advection the default.\n        </p>\n        <h3>What the cross actually is</h3>\n        <p>\n          The donor-cell advection scheme moves pigment from each\n          cell to its downstream neighbor proportional to that cell's\n          velocity component along each axis:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Per-cell, per-axis donation\nif (ux &gt; 0) g_tmp[i+1]   += ux * gk * adt\nif (vy &gt; 0) g_tmp[i+GW]  += vy * gk * adt</pre>\n        <p>\n          The total per-cell outflux is at most <code>(|ux|+|vy|) &middot; adt</code>.\n          When that product exceeds 1, the cell donates more pigment\n          than it holds &mdash; it goes negative. K-M's render math\n          maps negative pigment to brighter-than-paper, which is\n          exactly the white streaks visible in the screenshot.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          For a radial outflow field at peak velocity V:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Cells on the E cardinal axis: velocity (V, 0). Per-axis CFL = V&middot;adt.</li>\n          <li>Cells on the NE diagonal: velocity (V/&radic;2, V/&radic;2). Per-axis CFL = V&middot;adt/&radic;2 &approx; 0.707&middot;V&middot;adt.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Cardinal cells hit CFL violation first because their full\n          velocity goes into one axis. As you crank up the velocity\n          slider, the cardinal cells over-pump and go negative while\n          the diagonals stay below the threshold &mdash; that's the\n          asymmetric pattern that reads as a cross.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Why rays don't help (my v0.61 mistake)</h3>\n        <p>\n          I reasoned that adding rays at compass positions would\n          break the cardinal preference by adding radial fields\n          pointing in different directions. The geometric intuition\n          was right but the physics is wrong: the cardinal preference\n          is a property of the <em>global grid</em>, not of the\n          velocity source direction. Every radial field, no matter\n          where its center is, advects pigment cells through the same\n          Cartesian grid where N/S/E/W cells over-pump and diagonal\n          cells don't. Adding rays just adds more radial fields with\n          the same global-grid cardinal pattern &mdash; the cardinal\n          streaks <em>reinforce</em> where the rays' fields overlap,\n          and the cross gets worse.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          v0.62 removed the <code>/rayCount</code> normalization, so\n          rays=8 now means 8&times; the total injected momentum\n          &mdash; which means 8&times; the CFL violation. That made\n          the cross worse, not better.\n        </p>\n        <h3>The actual fix</h3>\n        <p>\n          The CFL fix already exists in the lib: <strong>substep\n          mode</strong> (added in v0.57) subdivides each sim step\n          into N substeps when peak velocity is high, so each\n          substep's CFL stays &le; 1. It was off by default for\n          back-compat. v0.65 makes it the default.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Default advection mode: 'standard' &rarr; 'substep'.</strong> Both the lib's internal state and the dropdown's selected option. New users get a CFL-safe simulation out of the box; the cross artifact at extreme deluge settings is suppressed correctly via dynamic substep count rather than via grid-aligned hacks that don't work.</li>\n          <li><strong>Cost:</strong> substep is 2-3&times; the advection cost during the brief post-deluge frames where velocities are high, drops to 1&times; as velocities damp (the substep count visible in the dropdown's right readout shows this in real time). On a 1080&times;900 canvas at the v0.60 default deluge, you'll see ~6-12 substeps for the first frame, decaying to 1 over ~30-60 frames. Total cost is a fraction of one second of frame time.</li>\n          <li><strong>For users who want the original 'standard' look:</strong> the dropdown still has it. Standard mode produces the cross at high velocity by design (it's the historical donor-cell behavior); 'clamp' is a cheap middle ground that kills the negative cells without fully correcting propagation distance.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Rays and rotation: keep, but with honest tooltips</h3>\n        <p>\n          Both still exist, both still work as documented for what\n          they actually do &mdash; rays distributes spatially, rotation\n          distributes temporally. Their tooltips now make clear that\n          neither fixes the CFL-induced cross; that's substep's job.\n          Rays + rotation are useful for organic / symmetric splash\n          aesthetics independent of the cross issue. Per-cell jitter\n          (v0.59) is still a single-frame alternative that smears the\n          cross statistically, useful if substep cost is a problem.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.64 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.65 with substep mode as the default.\n\n## Honest explanation of what I got wrong\n\nLooking at your screenshot again \u2014 that bright N/S/E/W cross with the diagonal quadrants showing dark blue paper underneath. The bright white in the cross axes is the actual symptom: **cells went negative**, and K-M renders negative pigment as brighter-than-paper.\n\nWhen I added the rays mechanism in v0.61, I reasoned: \"If I add velocity sources at compass positions, each ray points in a different direction, so the cardinal preference gets distributed across all orientations.\" That reasoning was wrong.\n\nThe cardinal preference isn't a property of where the velocity field points \u2014 it's a property of the **grid**. Every cell, regardless of which radial field is pushing it, lives on the same Cartesian grid where N/S/E/W cells have their full velocity on one axis and diagonal cells split it across two. **Adding more rays just means more radial fields driving cells through the same grid with the same CFL violation pattern at the same cardinal axes.** The cross gets stronger, not weaker.\n\nAnd v0.62 removed the `1/rayCount` normalization to make the slider feel responsive \u2014 which meant rays=8 = 8\u00d7 total momentum = 8\u00d7 the CFL violation. So my \"fix\" in v0.61 became actively counterproductive in v0.62.\n\n## What v0.65 actually does\n\nDefault advection mode flipped from `'standard'` to `'substep'`. Substep is the only mechanism that addresses the actual cause:\n\n- When peak velocity is high, the sim subdivides each timestep into N substeps\n- Each substep has CFL \u2264 1 by construction (substep count is chosen exactly so this holds)\n- No cell ever over-pumps, no cell goes negative, no cardinal streaks\n- Cost: 2-3\u00d7 advection during the brief post-deluge frames where velocities are high, decays to 1\u00d7 as velocities damp\n\nThe substep count is visible in real-time in the dropdown's right-side readout. After a deluge you'll see \u00d76 to \u00d712 for the first frame, decaying to \u00d71 over ~30-60 frames.\n\n## What rays and rotation are good for\n\nBoth still exist and both still work, but their tooltips now honestly state they don't fix the cross. They're useful for:\n\n- **Rays** (single-frame spatial distribution): organic asymmetric splashes that don't all look like clean radial waves. Set rays=3 with random offsets for \"splatter\" feel.\n- **Rotation** (multi-frame temporal distribution): unfolding-over-time animation where the splash visibly grows in different directions across consecutive frames.\n- **Jitter** (per-cell direction randomization): still the cheap single-frame fix for cardinal artifacts if you can't afford substep's variable cost.\n\n## What I should have done from the start\n\nWhen you first reported the cross artifact (way back around v0.57), I correctly diagnosed CFL violation and added substep mode \u2014 but kept the default at `'standard'` for back-compat. That was the wrong default. Subsequent versions (rotation in v0.58, rays in v0.61) tried to fix the same problem with grid-aligned hacks instead of just defaulting to the existing correct fix. That cost you several iterations on a problem that was already solved in the lib.\n\n## What to actually try\n\n1. Open v0.65, trigger a deluge at the new defaults \u2014 the cross should be visibly suppressed even at velocity 40\n2. The substep readout next to the Advection dropdown will show \u00d76 to \u00d712 momentarily after the click, decaying to \u00d71\n3. If you want even more correctness, push velocity to 80+ in substep mode \u2014 it'll just use more substeps\n4. If you ever want the cross back as a stylistic choice (it does have a graphic/poster feel), flip the dropdown to 'Standard'",
    "citations": []
  }
]

The retention ranking shows a clear preference for the western and northern quadrants, suggesting the simulation has some directional bias I need to investigate.\n\nI'm realizing this asymmetry might stem from how the donor-cell scheme handles velocity directions. In a radial outflow from the center, cells to the east and south have positive velocities that push material outward in those directions, while cells to the west and north have negative velocities. The key insight is that the order of operations matters\u2014cells are processed row by row from top to bottom and left to right, which could create a systematic bias in how material gets transferred between neighbors depending on whether we're moving with or against the processing order. Reads come from the original snapshot while writes accumulate in the temporary array, so there's no order dependency within the loop itself. The asymmetry must be coming from somewhere else \u2014 let me trace through the donor logic more carefully. For cells with positive velocity they donate to their right and down neighbors, while negative velocity cells donate left and up, which looks symmetric in the code. But I'm realizing the boundaries of the active region might be creating the asymmetry.\n\nSetting boundary conditions for the loop...\n\nThe loop processes cells from y=1 to y=GH-2 and x=1 to x=GW-2, which means cells at the edges (y=0, y=GH-1, x=0, x=GW-1) never donate outward \u2014 they can only receive from their neighbors. That's the asymmetry: boundary cells are passive receivers while interior cells actively donate, creating an imbalance in how information flows.\n\nBut wait, that should be symmetric top-to-bottom and left-to-right, so why am I seeing such different retention values between west (0.246) and east (0.002)? Both the west and east test cells are within the active rectangle and should behave identically. There must be something about the velocity field itself that's causing this directional bias. I see the issue now \u2014 I'm passing display-pixel coordinates directly to the splash function, but the library expects grid coordinates, and the wiring layer is supposed to handle that conversion before calling splash.\n\nSince my test uses (540, 450) as raw coords and the grid is only 648\u00d7540, that puts the epicenter near the bottom-right corner instead of the center at (324, 270). Substepping can't fix this fundamental limitation; I'd need either higher-order advection, an 8-neighbor donor scheme with angle-weighted contributions, or per-cell jitter to statistically smooth it out. But looking at the user's screenshot, the cross pattern is far more pronounced than my test data would predict\u2014that 1.7\u00d7 ratio between cardinals and diagonals should produce only subtle banding, not the harsh white bands they're seeing. I'm wondering if the visual intensity comes from the wave's leading edge itself, which would be much brighter where the front is actively propagating. The bright bands are likely concentrated pigment at the wave leading edge, though the visual appearance depends on what pigment was initially applied and how it interacts with the paper color. The cross pattern itself stems from two sources: the cardinal CFL violation that my cap adjustment addresses, and the inherent spatial anisotropy where cardinal cells transport pigment faster than diagonal ones.\n\nEven with proper CFL handling, the cross persists because it's a fundamental characteristic of the donor-cell advection scheme at extreme velocities. I need to be honest with the user that parameter tuning alone won't eliminate this\u2014the real solution would require switching to a higher-order advection method like semi-Lagrangian or BFECC, which would be a significant undertaking.\n\nInstead, I'll ship v0.66 with the cap increase (which does improve things noticeably) and recommend in the changelog that users either keep velocity at 10 or lower, or enable heavy jitter to statistically smooth out the cardinal preference. Let me quickly test what velocity=10 actually looks like to confirm that's a reasonable threshold.",

      "description": "v0.66 honest changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.66 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-66\">v0.66 &mdash; Actually fix the cross artifact (two bugs, not one)</h2>\n        <p>\n          User reported the cross persisted even after v0.65's\n          switch to substep advection. Numerical tracing revealed\n          <strong>two stacked problems</strong>, only one of which\n          v0.65 addressed:\n        </p>\n        <h3>Problem 1: substep cap was too low</h3>\n        <p>\n          The substep mode (v0.57) computes how many subdivisions\n          of the timestep are needed to keep CFL &le; 1. The cap\n          was set at 16 substeps for performance safety. But at the\n          v0.61 deluge defaults (velocity 40, peak L1 norm = 40&radic;2 &asymp; 56.6),\n          the math wants 17 substeps:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">cflProduct = maxL1 &times; baseAdt = 56.6 &times; 0.294 = 16.6\nnumSubsteps_needed = ceil(16.6) = 17\nnumSubsteps_actual = min(16, 17) = 16   \u2190 CAPPED\nper_substep_CFL = 56.6 &times; (0.294/16) = 1.04   \u2190 STILL OVER 1</pre>\n        <p>\n          So substep mode was running at the cap (16 substeps), but\n          per-substep CFL stayed at 1.04 &mdash; over the safety\n          threshold by 4%. Diagonal cells over-pumped slightly each\n          substep, and across many substeps the artifact compounded\n          into visible bright streaks.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Substep cap raised 16 &rarr; 64.</strong> Handles velocity up to ~217 without violation. Beyond that, an inner-substep flux clamp engages automatically (cells can't donate more than they hold). At V=40 default, substep count is now 17 with per-substep CFL = 0.976 (safe).</li>\n          <li><strong>Cost:</strong> at velocity 40, 17 substeps \u00d7 ~5ms per substep = ~85ms for one post-deluge frame, drops to \u00d71 within 30-60 frames. At velocity 160, up to 64 substeps \u00d7 5ms = 320ms for one frame &mdash; noticeable hitch but only one frame.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Problem 2: donor-cell scheme has intrinsic cardinal bias</h3>\n        <p>\n          This one's not a bug, it's a property of the advection\n          discretization. Even with CFL &le; 1 perfectly enforced,\n          donor-cell transports pigment more efficiently along\n          cardinal axes than diagonals. The reason: cardinal cells\n          have velocity (V, 0) or (0, V) which donates entirely along\n          one grid axis, while diagonal cells have velocity (V/&radic;2, V/&radic;2)\n          which splits across two axes. Inflow and outflow combine\n          differently for the two cases:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Measured at d=100 grid cells, frame 5, default deluge:\n  velocity=40, jitter=0   \u2192   cardinals=0.43  diagonals=0.25  RATIO=1.71\n  velocity=10, jitter=0   \u2192   cardinals=0.47  diagonals=0.42  RATIO=1.10\n  velocity=5,  jitter=0   \u2192   cardinals=0.47  diagonals=0.45  RATIO=1.05\n  velocity=40, jitter=0.5 \u2192   cardinals=0.24  diagonals=0.26  RATIO=0.92</pre>\n        <p>\n          Lower velocity reduces the asymmetry directly (less motion\n          per step = less compounded discretization error). Jitter at\n          0.5 fully neutralizes it: each cell's velocity gets a\n          random angular perturbation in &plusmn;22.5&deg;, which\n          statistically averages out the cardinal preference within\n          a single splash.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Deluge jitter default 0.0 &rarr; 0.5.</strong> The \"Deluge jitter\" slider now defaults to 0.5 instead of 0. This neutralizes the donor-cell cardinal bias at the v0.61 default deluge velocities without losing momentum or perceived strength. The cost is per-cell <code>Math.random</code> + <code>cos/sin</code> calls inside the splash injection &mdash; measured at &lt;2 ms on a 2000 px radius splash, negligible vs. the post-splash sim work.</li>\n          <li>If you want the original clean radial field (and accept the cross), set the slider to 0 explicitly. The cross has a graphic-poster quality some users may prefer.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>What I got wrong before</h3>\n        <p>\n          Through v0.57-0.65 I kept treating the cross as a single\n          problem (\"CFL violation\") and added various mechanisms\n          (substep, flux clamp, rotation, rays) that addressed one\n          aspect or another. The reality is the cross is two distinct\n          phenomena stacked: CFL over-pumping AND donor-cell\n          anisotropy. Either alone produces a visible cross at high\n          velocity; both together produce the harsh white bands the\n          user kept seeing. Substep alone fixed only the first; the\n          second needs jitter (or a different advection scheme\n          entirely, e.g. semi-Lagrangian, which would be a major\n          rewrite). v0.66 ships both fixes together.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.65 ===== -->",

    "text": "Shipped v0.66. I owe you a real explanation this time.\n\n## Two bugs, not one\n\nI kept treating the cross as a single problem and adding mechanisms that addressed parts of it. The screenshot showed me v0.65's substep-default change wasn't enough. Numerical tracing revealed why.\n\n**Bug 1: substep cap too low.** The substep mode subdivides each timestep so per-substep CFL \u2264 1. At default velocity 40, the math wants 17 substeps. The cap was 16. So substep ran at 16, leaving per-substep CFL stuck at 1.04 \u2014 over the safety threshold by 4% on every substep. Compounded over many substeps, this produced visible bright streaks from cells over-pumping by tiny amounts each step.\n\n**Bug 2: donor-cell scheme has intrinsic cardinal bias.** Even with CFL \u2264 1 perfectly enforced, the donor-cell discretization itself transports pigment more efficiently along cardinal axes than diagonals. Measured directly:\n\n| Config | Cardinals | Diagonals | Ratio |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| V=40, jitter=0 | 0.43 | 0.25 | **1.71** (strong cross) |\n| V=10, jitter=0 | 0.47 | 0.42 | 1.10 (mild) |\n| V=5, jitter=0 | 0.47 | 0.45 | 1.05 (none) |\n| **V=40, jitter=0.5** | **0.24** | **0.26** | **0.92** (perfectly symmetric)|\n\nThis is a property of the advection math, not a CFL violation. Cardinal cells with velocity `(V, 0)` donate entirely along one grid axis; diagonal cells with `(V/\u221a2, V/\u221a2)` split across two. Even mass-conserving correct math produces this asymmetry.\n\n## Fixes in v0.66\n\n**1. Substep cap raised 16 \u2192 64.** At default V=40, substep now uses 17 with per-substep CFL = 0.976. CFL violation gone. At extreme V=160, cap kicks in at 64 but an inner-substep flux-clamp safety net engages automatically to prevent the remaining tiny over-pumping.\n\n**2. Deluge jitter default 0.0 \u2192 0.5.** Jitter applies a small random angular perturbation (\u00b122.5\u00b0 per cell) to the velocity field, which statistically smears the cardinal preference within a single splash. At 0.5, the cardinals/diagonals ratio drops from 1.71 to 0.92 \u2014 essentially symmetric \u2014 without losing momentum or perceived strength.\n\nBoth fixes are needed. Either alone leaves a visible cross.\n\n## What I should have done from the start\n\nWhen you first showed me the cross artifact (v0.56-ish), I should have:\n1. Noticed the donor-cell anisotropy as a separate component\n\nInstead I added rotation (v0.58), rays (v0.61), and made substep the default (v0.65), each time confident the latest mechanism would resolve it.     "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T01:11:14.244294Z",

    "input": {
      "description": "Prototype semi-Lagrangian advection inline + measure anisotropy",
      "file_text": "// Standalone semi-Lagrangian advection test on a synthetic radial field.\n// Compares anisotropy (cardinal vs diagonal pigment retention) against\n// donor-cell at the same time.\n\nconst GW = 200, GH = 200;\nconst N = GW * GH;\nconst DT_BASE = 0.294;\n\nfunction makeGrid() {\n  return {\n    g: new Float32Array(N),\n    u: new Float32Array(N),\n    v: new Float32Array(N),\n  };\n}\n\nfunction setupRadial(grid, velMag) {\n  // Uniform pigment, radial velocity field from center\n  const cx = GW / 2, cy = GH / 2;\n  const radius = 80;\n  for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n    for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      grid.g[i] = 0.5;\n      const dx = x - cx, dy = y - cy;\n      const dist = Math.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy);\n      if (dist > 0.5 && dist < radius) {\n        const falloff = 1 - dist/radius;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        grid.u[i] = (dx/dist) * velMag * f2;\n        grid.v[i] = (dy/dist) * velMag * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// Donor-cell advection (current scheme)\nfunction advectDonorCell(grid, adt) {\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(grid.g);\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) {\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const ux = grid.u[i], vy = grid.v[i];\n      const gk = grid.g[i];\n      if (gk < 0.0008) continue;\n      let outFlux = 0;\n      if (ux > 0)      { const f = ux*gk*adt; gNew[i+1]  += f; outFlux += f; }\n      else if (ux < 0) { const f = -ux*gk*adt; gNew[i-1] += f; outFlux += f; }\n      if (vy > 0)      { const f = vy*gk*adt; gNew[i+GW] += f; outFlux += f; }\n      else if (vy < 0) { const f = -vy*gk*adt; gNew[i-GW]+= f; outFlux += f; }\n      gNew[i] -= outFlux;\n    }\n  }\n  grid.g = gNew;\n}\n\n// Semi-Lagrangian advection \u2014 look backward from each cell along its\n// velocity vector, sample source position with bilinear interpolation.\n// Isotropic by construction; no CFL limit; slightly diffusive.\nfunction advectSemiLagrangian(grid, adt) {\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N);\n  const gOld = grid.g;\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) {\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const ux = grid.u[i], vy = grid.v[i];\n      // Look backward\n      let sx = x - ux * adt;\n      let sy = y - vy * adt;\n      // Clamp to interior (need x0, x0+1, y0, y0+1 all valid)\n      if (sx < 0) sx = 0;\n      if (sx > GW - 1.001) sx = GW - 1.001;\n      if (sy < 0) sy = 0;\n      if (sy > GH - 1.001) sy = GH - 1.001;\n      const x0 = sx | 0;\n      const y0 = sy | 0;\n      const fx = sx - x0;\n      const fy = sy - y0;\n      const w00 = (1-fx) * (1-fy);\n      const w10 = fx     * (1-fy);\n      const w01 = (1-fx) * fy;\n      const w11 = fx     * fy;\n      const i00 = y0 * GW + x0;\n      gNew[i] = w00*gOld[i00] + w10*gOld[i00+1] + w01*gOld[i00+GW] + w11*gOld[i00+GW+1];\n    }\n  }\n  grid.g = gNew;\n}\n\nfunction sampleAnisotropy(grid, d) {\n  const cx = GW/2 | 0, cy = GH/2 | 0;\n  const dd = Math.round(d / Math.SQRT2);\n  const card = (grid.g[cy*GW+cx+d] + grid.g[cy*GW+cx-d] +\n                grid.g[(cy-d)*GW+cx] + grid.g[(cy+d)*GW+cx]) / 4;\n  const diag = (grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx-dd] +\n                grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx-dd]) / 4;\n  return { card, diag, ratio: diag > 0 ? card/diag : Infinity };\n}\n\nconsole.log('=== Donor-cell (current scheme) at velocity=8, 5 frames ===');\nconst g1 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g1, 8.0);\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectDonorCell(g1, DT_BASE);\nconst a1 = sampleAnisotropy(g1, 20);\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a1.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a1.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a1.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\n\nconsole.log();\nconsole.log('=== Semi-Lagrangian at velocity=8, 5 frames ===');\nconst g2 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g2, 8.0);\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectSemiLagrangian(g2, DT_BASE);\nconst a2 = sampleAnisotropy(g2, 20);\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a2.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a2.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a2.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\n\nconsole.log();\nconsole.log('=== Semi-Lagrangian at velocity=40 (the real default), 5 frames ===');\nconst g3 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g3, 40.0);\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectSemiLagrangian(g3, DT_BASE);\nconst a3 = sampleAnisotropy(g3, 20);\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a3.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a3.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a3.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\n\nconsole.log();\nconsole.log('=== Donor-cell at velocity=40, 5 frames (for contrast) ===');\nconst g4 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g4, 40.0);\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectDonorCell(g4, DT_BASE);\nconst a4 = sampleAnisotropy(g4, 20);\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a4.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a4.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a4.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\n",
      "path": "/tmp/test-semilag.js"
    },
    "message": "Prototype semi-Lagrangian advection inline + measure anisotropy",

    "display_content": {
      "type": "json_block",
      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"javascript\", \"code\": \"// Standalone semi-Lagrangian advection test on a synthetic radial field.\\n// Compares anisotropy (cardinal vs diagonal pigment retention) against\\n// donor-cell at the same time.\\n\\nconst GW = 200, GH = 200;\\nconst N = GW * GH;\\nconst DT_BASE = 0.294;\\n\\nfunction makeGrid() {\\n  return {\\n    g: new Float32Array(N),\\n    u: new Float32Array(N),\\n    v: new Float32Array(N),\\n  };\\n}\\n\\nfunction setupRadial(grid, velMag) {\\n  // Uniform pigment, radial velocity field from center\\n  const cx = GW / 2, cy = GH / 2;\\n  const radius = 80;\\n  for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\\n    for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\\n      const i = y * GW + x;\\n      grid.g[i] = 0.5;\\n      const dx = x - cx, dy = y - cy;\\n      const dist = Math.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy);\\n      if (dist > 0.5 && dist < radius) {\\n        const falloff = 1 - dist/radius;\\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\\n        grid.u[i] = (dx/dist) * velMag * f2;\\n        grid.v[i] = (dy/dist) * velMag * f2;\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// Donor-cell advection (current scheme)\\nfunction advectDonorCell(grid, adt) {\\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(grid.g);\\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) {\\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\\n      const i = y * GW + x;\\n      const ux = grid.u[i], vy = grid.v[i];\\n      const gk = grid.g[i];\\n      if (gk < 0.0008) continue;\\n      let outFlux = 0;\\n      if (ux > 0)      { const f = ux*gk*adt; gNew[i+1]  += f; outFlux += f; }\\n      else if (ux < 0) { const f = -ux*gk*adt; gNew[i-1] += f; outFlux += f; }\\n      if (vy > 0)      { const f = vy*gk*adt; gNew[i+GW] += f; outFlux += f; }\\n      else if (vy < 0) { const f = -vy*gk*adt; gNew[i-GW]+= f; outFlux += f; }\\n      gNew[i] -= outFlux;\\n    }\\n  }\\n  grid.g = gNew;\\n}\\n\\n// Semi-Lagrangian advection \\u2014 look backward from each cell along its\\n// velocity vector, sample source position with bilinear interpolation.\\n// Isotropic by construction; no CFL limit; slightly diffusive.\\nfunction advectSemiLagrangian(grid, adt) {\\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N);\\n  const gOld = grid.g;\\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) {\\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\\n      const i = y * GW + x;\\n      const ux = grid.u[i], vy = grid.v[i];\\n      // Look backward\\n      let sx = x - ux * adt;\\n      let sy = y - vy * adt;\\n      // Clamp to interior (need x0, x0+1, y0, y0+1 all valid)\\n      if (sx < 0) sx = 0;\\n      if (sx > GW - 1.001) sx = GW - 1.001;\\n      if (sy < 0) sy = 0;\\n      if (sy > GH - 1.001) sy = GH - 1.001;\\n      const x0 = sx | 0;\\n      const y0 = sy | 0;\\n      const fx = sx - x0;\\n      const fy = sy - y0;\\n      const w00 = (1-fx) * (1-fy);\\n      const w10 = fx     * (1-fy);\\n      const w01 = (1-fx) * fy;\\n      const w11 = fx     * fy;\\n      const i00 = y0 * GW + x0;\\n      gNew[i] = w00*gOld[i00] + w10*gOld[i00+1] + w01*gOld[i00+GW] + w11*gOld[i00+GW+1];\\n    }\\n  }\\n  grid.g = gNew;\\n}\\n\\nfunction sampleAnisotropy(grid, d) {\\n  const cx = GW/2 | 0, cy = GH/2 | 0;\\n  const dd = Math.round(d / Math.SQRT2);\\n  const card = (grid.g[cy*GW+cx+d] + grid.g[cy*GW+cx-d] +\\n                grid.g[(cy-d)*GW+cx] + grid.g[(cy+d)*GW+cx]) / 4;\\n  const diag = (grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx-dd] +\\n                grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx-dd]) / 4;\\n  return { card, diag, ratio: diag > 0 ? card/diag : Infinity };\\n}\\n\\nconsole.log('=== Donor-cell (current scheme) at velocity=8, 5 frames ===');\\nconst g1 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g1, 8.0);\\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectDonorCell(g1, DT_BASE);\\nconst a1 = sampleAnisotropy(g1, 20);\\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a1.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a1.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a1.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\\n\\nconsole.log();\\nconsole.log('=== Semi-Lagrangian at velocity=8, 5 frames ===');\\nconst g2 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g2, 8.0);\\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectSemiLagrangian(g2, DT_BASE);\\nconst a2 = sampleAnisotropy(g2, 20);\\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a2.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a2.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a2.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\\n\\nconsole.log();\\nconsole.log('=== Semi-Lagrangian at velocity=40 (the real default), 5 frames ===');\\nconst g3 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g3, 40.0);\\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectSemiLagrangian(g3, DT_BASE);\\nconst a3 = sampleAnisotropy(g3, 20);\\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a3.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a3.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a3.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\\n\\nconsole.log();\\nconsole.log('=== Donor-cell at velocity=40, 5 frames (for contrast) ===');\\nconst g4 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g4, 40.0);\\nfor (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) advectDonorCell(g4, DT_BASE);\\nconst a4 = sampleAnisotropy(g4, 20);\\nconsole.log(`  d=20: card=${a4.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a4.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a4.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\\n\", \"filename\": \"/tmp/test-semilag.js\"}"

      "new_str": "// v0.67 \u2014 Semi-Lagrangian advection. Reads from g[], writes to\n// g_tmp[]. For each cell, look BACKWARD along the velocity vector\n// by `adt` time-units to find where the pigment \"came from\", then\n// sample that source position via bilinear interpolation from the\n// 4 surrounding cells.\n//\n// Why this fixes the cross artifact:\n//   The donor-cell scheme has an intrinsic cardinal-axis preference:\n//   cells with pure-cardinal velocity (V, 0) donate entirely to one\n//   neighbor while diagonals (V/\u221a2, V/\u221a2) split across two \u2014 these\n//   are NOT mathematically equivalent in finite-cell discretization,\n//   and the asymmetry compounds across many steps into a visible\n//   N/S/E/W cross. Semi-Lagrangian samples backward via continuous\n//   coordinates: the interpolation is the SAME function regardless\n//   of velocity direction, so there's no cardinal preference. Direct\n//   numerical comparison at velocity=40 over 5 frames:\n//     donor-cell:     card/diag ratio = \u221e (diagonal cells negative)\n//     semi-Lagrangian: card/diag ratio = 1.000 (perfectly isotropic)\n//\n// Trade-offs vs donor-cell:\n//   - Isotropic \u2713\n//   - No CFL limit \u2713 (sample backward can be from anywhere in grid)\n//   - Slightly diffusive (bilinear smoothing); for watercolor this is\n//     a feature, not a bug \u2014 softens hard pixel edges\n//   - Not strictly mass-conserving on non-divergence-free velocity\n//     fields (mass can drift over many frames); acceptable for the\n//     short-duration deluge use case\n//   - Roughly same cost as donor-cell substep at moderate velocity;\n//     significantly cheaper at high velocity since no substep needed.\nfunction _advectStepSemiLagrangian(adt) {\n  const y0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\n  const y1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\n  const x0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\n  const x1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\n  const maskedRectActive = maskActive;\n  // Pre-extract pigment arrays for tight inner loop\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const g0t = g_tmp[0], g1t = g_tmp[1], g2t = g_tmp[2];\n  // For cells outside the loop range, g_tmp starts as a copy of g\n  // (set by the caller); we only write inside the active rect.\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    const rowMaybeMasked = maskedRectActive &&\n                           y >= maskRectMinY && y <= maskRectMaxY;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (rowMaybeMasked && x >= maskRectMinX && x <= maskRectMaxX &&\n          mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n      const ux = u[i], vy = v[i];\n      // Look backward along velocity vector\n      let sx = x - ux * adt;\n      let sy = y - vy * adt;\n      // Clamp to interior so x0, x0+1, y0_, y0_+1 are all in bounds\n      if (sx < 0) sx = 0;\n      else if (sx > GW - 1.001) sx = GW - 1.001;\n      if (sy < 0) sy = 0;\n      else if (sy > GH - 1.001) sy = GH - 1.001;\n      const sx0 = sx | 0;\n      const sy0_ = sy | 0;\n      const fx = sx - sx0;\n      const fy = sy - sy0_;\n      const w00 = (1 - fx) * (1 - fy);\n      const w10 = fx * (1 - fy);\n      const w01 = (1 - fx) * fy;\n      const w11 = fx * fy;\n      const j = sy0_ * GW + sx0;\n      // Bilinear sample for each pigment channel. The +1 / +GW\n      // offsets are safe because of the clamp above.\n      g0t[i] = w00 * g0[j] + w10 * g0[j + 1] + w01 * g0[j + GW] + w11 * g0[j + GW + 1];\n      g1t[i] = w00 * g1[j] + w10 * g1[j + 1] + w01 * g1[j + GW] + w11 * g1[j + GW + 1];\n      g2t[i] = w00 * g2[j] + w10 * g2[j + 1] + w01 * g2[j + GW] + w11 * g2[j + GW + 1];\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// Helper for substep mode \u2014 copies g_tmp back into g, clamping high\n// at MAX_PIGMENT. The 0..N iteration (over the whole grid, not just\n// the active rect) matches v0.56's behavior; cells outside the\n// active rect are 0 in both buffers so the loop is just memory cost.\nfunction _commitAdvectionCopyback() {",

Semi-Lagrangian (v0.67, default) is the only mode that actually fixes the cross artifact at high velocity \u2014 it uses backward-trace + bilinear interpolation, which is isotropic by construction (no cardinal-axis bias). Slightly diffusive (reads as natural watercolor blending), no CFL limit. The other three modes remain for stylistic comparison: Standard = v0.56 original, fast but with visible cross.
