    "id": "toolu_01RZLoUQrYaFBLxMUtXd5sfc",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "v0.67 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.67 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-67\">v0.67 &mdash; Semi-Lagrangian advection (real cross fix)</h2>\n        <p>\n          v0.66's \"jitter at 0.5\" workaround traded the cross\n          artifact for visible radial striations. Both are bad. The\n          correct fix &mdash; and the one I should have shipped four\n          versions ago &mdash; is to replace donor-cell pigment\n          advection with a semi-Lagrangian scheme, which is\n          isotropic by construction.\n        </p>\n        <h3>How semi-Lagrangian works</h3>\n        <p>\n          For each cell, the scheme looks <em>backward</em> along the\n          velocity vector by <code>adt</code> time-units to find where\n          the pigment \"came from\" this step, then samples that source\n          position via bilinear interpolation from the 4 surrounding\n          cells:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Per cell:\nsx = x - u[i] * adt\nsy = y - v[i] * adt\nx0 = floor(sx), y0 = floor(sy)\nfx = sx - x0,   fy = sy - y0\nnew_pigment[i] = (1-fx)(1-fy) * g[y0  ][x0  ]\n               + fx    (1-fy) * g[y0  ][x0+1]\n               + (1-fx)fy     * g[y0+1][x0  ]\n               + fx    fy     * g[y0+1][x0+1]</pre>\n        <p>\n          Why it's isotropic: the bilinear interpolation function\n          <em>doesn't know which direction is which</em>. It uses\n          continuous source coordinates and weights neighbors by\n          proximity. There's no per-axis donation logic, so no per-axis\n          asymmetry. Direct numerical comparison at velocity=40 over\n          5 frames at d=100 cells from the epicenter:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Mode        cardinals  diagonals  ratio (1.0 = isotropic)\n\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\nstandard    -4.554     -2.021     \u221e (negative cells = CFL violation)\nclamp        0.464      0.454     1.023 (cheap fix, mildly diffusive)\nsubstep      0.431      0.252     1.713 (still very anisotropic!)\nsemilag      0.471      0.471     1.001 (PERFECT)</pre>\n        <p>\n          Note that <strong>substep at ratio 1.713 is still\n          anisotropic</strong>. v0.65-0.66's belief that substep alone\n          fixed the cross was wrong. Substep eliminates CFL violation\n          (over-pumping \u2192 negative cells \u2192 bright streaks) but\n          <em>not</em> the donor-cell scheme's underlying cardinal\n          preference. That's why the v0.66 screenshot still had a\n          visible cross even at substep cap 64.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Trade-offs</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Isotropic \u2713</strong> \u2014 no cardinal preference. Ratio 1.001 in measurements.</li>\n          <li><strong>No CFL limit \u2713</strong> \u2014 bilinear sampling works for any velocity. The cell can sample from arbitrarily far back along the velocity vector.</li>\n          <li><strong>Slightly diffusive</strong> \u2014 bilinear interpolation smooths the field by averaging neighbor cells. For watercolor this is a <em>feature</em>: softens hard pixel edges, blends pigment naturally. The previous donor-cell scheme had a separate explicit diffusion step to compensate; semilag has it built in.</li>\n          <li><strong>Not strictly mass-conserving</strong> \u2014 on a non-divergence-free velocity field (which ours isn't, since we don't enforce \u2207\u00b7v=0), total mass can drift over many frames. For the short-duration deluge use case this is negligible; for ultra-long simulations would need correction. Donor-cell IS strictly conservative.</li>\n          <li><strong>Cost \u2248 substep at moderate velocity</strong>; significantly cheaper at high velocity since no substep needed. One pass through the active rect per frame, regardless of velocity.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Changes</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New \"Semi-Lagrangian\" advection mode</strong>. Selectable from the Advection dropdown; available via <code>wc.advectionMode('semilag')</code>.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Default advection mode: 'substep' &rarr; 'semilag'.</strong> Both the lib's internal state and the dropdown's selected option.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Deluge jitter default: 0.5 &rarr; 0.0.</strong> The v0.66 jitter-as-cross-fix workaround is no longer needed and was actively harmful (caused visible radial striations). Jitter remains available 0..1 for stylistic effects.</li>\n          <li>Three previous modes (standard, clamp, substep) remain for stylistic comparison. Standard at velocity 40 produces dramatic over-pumped cross; substep produces a softer but still anisotropic cross; clamp is the closest non-semilag mode visually.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>What I got wrong across versions 0.57-0.66</h3>\n        <p>\n          I added five mechanisms over nine version bumps trying to\n          fix the cross: flux clamp (v0.57), substep (v0.57),\n          rotation distribution (v0.58), spatial rays (v0.61),\n          per-cell jitter (v0.59 / 0.66). Each one addressed a\n          different facet of donor-cell weirdness, and each one was\n          subtly wrong in a different way. The numerical comparison\n          above shows clearly: <em>only semi-Lagrangian achieves\n          ratio 1.001</em>. The right fix was always to replace the\n          advection scheme entirely, not patch around it. I should\n          have shipped this in v0.57 instead of substep mode. Costs\n          you eight versions of trial-and-error on an issue that had\n          a standard textbook answer. Sorry about that.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.66 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.67. The actual fix this time.\n\n## What I should have done in v0.57\n\nThe cross artifact had a standard textbook answer the entire time: **replace donor-cell advection with semi-Lagrangian**. Donor-cell is inherently anisotropic on a Cartesian grid \u2014 cardinal cells donate entirely along one axis, diagonal cells split across two axes, and the asymmetry compounds. *No amount of CFL fixing, substepping, jittering, ray-distribution, or rotation can fix this* because the bias is intrinsic to the discretization itself.\n\nSemi-Lagrangian works completely differently:\n1. For each destination cell, compute where the pigment \"came from\" by going *backward* along the velocity vector\n2. Sample that source position via bilinear interpolation from the 4 surrounding cells\n\nThe interpolation function doesn't know which direction is which. It uses continuous coordinates and weights neighbors by proximity. There's no per-axis donation logic, so there's no per-axis asymmetry. Numerical comparison at velocity 40:\n\n| Mode | Cardinals | Diagonals | Ratio (1.0 = isotropic) |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| standard | -4.55 | -2.02 | \u221e (negative cells) |\n| clamp | 0.464 | 0.454 | 1.023 |\n| substep | 0.431 | 0.252 | **1.713 (still cross!)** |\n| **semilag** | 0.471 | 0.471 | **1.001 (perfect)** |\n\nNotice that substep ratio is 1.713 \u2014 still very anisotropic. v0.65-0.66's belief that substep fixed the cross was wrong. Substep fixes CFL violation (negative cells from over-pumping) but leaves the underlying cardinal preference. That's why your v0.66 screenshot still had a visible cross even with cap raised to 64.\n\n## Trade-offs\n\n- **Isotropic \u2713** \u2014 no cardinal preference\n- **No CFL limit \u2713** \u2014 works for any velocity, no substep needed\n- **Slightly diffusive** \u2014 bilinear interpolation smooths the field. For watercolor this reads as natural soft blending; the old donor-cell scheme actually needed an *explicit* diffusion step to compensate, semilag has it built in\n- **Not strictly mass-conserving** on non-divergence-free velocity fields. Mass can drift slowly over many frames. Negligible for the deluge use case; donor-cell was strictly conservative.\n\n## Defaults rolled back\n\n- Jitter default: **0.5 \u2192 0** (semilag fixes the cross, no workaround needed)\n- Advection: **substep \u2192 semilag**\n\nThe other three modes remain available for stylistic comparison. Standard at velocity 40 produces a dramatic over-pumped cross; substep produces a softer but still anisotropic cross; clamp is the closest non-semilag mode visually (ratio 1.023, almost as good as semilag).\n\n## Honest accounting\n\nFrom v0.57 (when you first reported the cross) to v0.66, I added five mechanisms attempting to fix it: flux clamp, substep, rotation distribution, spatial rays, per-cell jitter. Each one addressed a different facet and each one was subtly wrong. The real fix was always to replace the advection scheme \u2014 about 60 lines of code, the standard textbook answer for this exact problem in fluid simulation. I should have done it in v0.57. Costs you 10 versions of trial-and-error. Sorry.\n\n## Test\n\nClick \"Deluge Now\" at all defaults. The cross should be absent. The splash should look like a uniform radial wash \u2014 soft, symmetric, no visible streaks or bands.",

      "description": "Compare semi-Lagrangian density-preserving vs mass-conserving",
      "file_text": "// Compare semi-Lagrangian variants on radial outflow:\n//   1. Density-preserving (current v0.67) \u2014 pigment just sampled back\n//   2. Mass-conserving \u2014 scale by (1 - adt * div(v)), so divergent flow reduces density\n\nconst GW = 100, GH = 100, N = GW*GH, DT = 0.294;\n\nfunction makeGrid() {\n  return { g: new Float32Array(N), u: new Float32Array(N), v: new Float32Array(N) };\n}\nfunction setupRadial(grid, velMag) {\n  const cx = GW/2, cy = GH/2, radius = 40;\n  for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n    for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n      const i = y*GW+x;\n      grid.g[i] = 0.5;\n      const dx = x-cx, dy = y-cy, dist = Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy);\n      if (dist > 0.5 && dist < radius) {\n        const f2 = (1 - dist/radius) ** 2;\n        grid.u[i] = (dx/dist) * velMag * f2;\n        grid.v[i] = (dy/dist) * velMag * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// Density-preserving (v0.67 current)\nfunction semilagDensity(grid, adt) {\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N), gOld = grid.g;\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\n    const i = y*GW+x;\n    let sx = x - grid.u[i]*adt, sy = y - grid.v[i]*adt;\n    sx = Math.max(0, Math.min(GW-1.001, sx));\n    sy = Math.max(0, Math.min(GH-1.001, sy));\n    const x0 = sx|0, y0 = sy|0, fx = sx-x0, fy = sy-y0;\n    const j = y0*GW+x0;\n    gNew[i] = (1-fx)*(1-fy)*gOld[j] + fx*(1-fy)*gOld[j+1] +\n              (1-fx)*fy*gOld[j+GW] + fx*fy*gOld[j+GW+1];\n  }\n  grid.g = gNew;\n}\n\n// Mass-conserving \u2014 apply divergence correction\nfunction semilagMass(grid, adt) {\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N), gOld = grid.g;\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\n    const i = y*GW+x;\n    // Divergence via central difference\n    const div = (grid.u[i+1] - grid.u[i-1]) * 0.5 + (grid.v[i+GW] - grid.v[i-GW]) * 0.5;\n    // Area ratio: exp(-div*adt). For small div, ~ 1 - div*adt. Use full exp to handle large divergence (radial centers).\n    const areaRatio = Math.exp(-div * adt);\n    // Bilinear sample\n    let sx = x - grid.u[i]*adt, sy = y - grid.v[i]*adt;\n    sx = Math.max(0, Math.min(GW-1.001, sx));\n    sy = Math.max(0, Math.min(GH-1.001, sy));\n    const x0 = sx|0, y0 = sy|0, fx = sx-x0, fy = sy-y0;\n    const j = y0*GW+x0;\n    const sample = (1-fx)*(1-fy)*gOld[j] + fx*(1-fy)*gOld[j+1] +\n                   (1-fx)*fy*gOld[j+GW] + fx*fy*gOld[j+GW+1];\n    gNew[i] = sample * areaRatio;\n  }\n  grid.g = gNew;\n}\n\nfunction sampleRadial(grid) {\n  const cx = GW/2|0, cy = GH/2|0;\n  const result = [];\n  for (const d of [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35]) {\n    // Average over 4 cardinals + 4 diagonals\n    const dd = Math.round(d/Math.SQRT2);\n    let sum = 0, cnt = 0;\n    if (d === 0) { sum = grid.g[cy*GW+cx]; cnt = 1; }\n    else {\n      sum = grid.g[cy*GW+cx+d] + grid.g[cy*GW+cx-d] +\n            grid.g[(cy-d)*GW+cx] + grid.g[(cy+d)*GW+cx] +\n            grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx-dd] +\n            grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx-dd];\n      cnt = 8;\n    }\n    result.push({ d, avg: (sum/cnt).toFixed(3) });\n  }\n  return result;\n}\n\nconsole.log('Initial: pigment = 0.5 uniform everywhere\\n');\n\nconsole.log('=== DENSITY-preserving semi-Lagrangian (current v0.67) ===');\nconst g1 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g1, 8);\nfor (let f = 0; f < 10; f++) semilagDensity(g1, DT);\nconst r1 = sampleRadial(g1);\nconsole.log('After 10 frames:');\nconsole.log(r1.map(s => `  d=${String(s.d).padStart(2)}: ${s.avg}`).join('\\n'));\nconsole.log('Visual: nearly uniform 0.5 \u2014 no \"lift and reveal\" effect\\n');\n\nconsole.log('=== MASS-conserving semi-Lagrangian (with div correction) ===');\nconst g2 = makeGrid();\nsetupRadial(g2, 8);\nfor (let f = 0; f < 10; f++) semilagMass(g2, DT);\nconst r2 = sampleRadial(g2);\nconsole.log('After 10 frames:');\nconsole.log(r2.map(s => `  d=${String(s.d).padStart(2)}: ${s.avg}`).join('\\n'));\nconsole.log('Visual: center cleared (lift!), pigment piled at edges\\n');\n",

    "message": "Compare semi-Lagrangian density-preserving vs mass-conserving",
    "integration_name": null,

    "context": null,
    "display_content": {
      "type": "json_block",
      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"javascript\", \"code\": \"// Compare semi-Lagrangian variants on radial outflow:\\n//   1. Density-preserving (current v0.67) \\u2014 pigment just sampled back\\n//   2. Mass-conserving \\u2014 scale by (1 - adt * div(v)), so divergent flow reduces density\\n\\nconst GW = 100, GH = 100, N = GW*GH, DT = 0.294;\\n\\nfunction makeGrid() {\\n  return { g: new Float32Array(N), u: new Float32Array(N), v: new Float32Array(N) };\\n}\\nfunction setupRadial(grid, velMag) {\\n  const cx = GW/2, cy = GH/2, radius = 40;\\n  for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\\n    for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\\n      const i = y*GW+x;\\n      grid.g[i] = 0.5;\\n      const dx = x-cx, dy = y-cy, dist = Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy);\\n      if (dist > 0.5 && dist < radius) {\\n        const f2 = (1 - dist/radius) ** 2;\\n        grid.u[i] = (dx/dist) * velMag * f2;\\n        grid.v[i] = (dy/dist) * velMag * f2;\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// Density-preserving (v0.67 current)\\nfunction semilagDensity(grid, adt) {\\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N), gOld = grid.g;\\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\\n    const i = y*GW+x;\\n    let sx = x - grid.u[i]*adt, sy = y - grid.v[i]*adt;\\n    sx = Math.max(0, Math.min(GW-1.001, sx));\\n    sy = Math.max(0, Math.min(GH-1.001, sy));\\n    const x0 = sx|0, y0 = sy|0, fx = sx-x0, fy = sy-y0;\\n    const j = y0*GW+x0;\\n    gNew[i] = (1-fx)*(1-fy)*gOld[j] + fx*(1-fy)*gOld[j+1] +\\n              (1-fx)*fy*gOld[j+GW] + fx*fy*gOld[j+GW+1];\\n  }\\n  grid.g = gNew;\\n}\\n\\n// Mass-conserving \\u2014 apply divergence correction\\nfunction semilagMass(grid, adt) {\\n  const gNew = new Float32Array(N), gOld = grid.g;\\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH-1; y++) for (let x = 1; x < GW-1; x++) {\\n    const i = y*GW+x;\\n    // Divergence via central difference\\n    const div = (grid.u[i+1] - grid.u[i-1]) * 0.5 + (grid.v[i+GW] - grid.v[i-GW]) * 0.5;\\n    // Area ratio: exp(-div*adt). For small div, ~ 1 - div*adt. Use full exp to handle large divergence (radial centers).\\n    const areaRatio = Math.exp(-div * adt);\\n    // Bilinear sample\\n    let sx = x - grid.u[i]*adt, sy = y - grid.v[i]*adt;\\n    sx = Math.max(0, Math.min(GW-1.001, sx));\\n    sy = Math.max(0, Math.min(GH-1.001, sy));\\n    const x0 = sx|0, y0 = sy|0, fx = sx-x0, fy = sy-y0;\\n    const j = y0*GW+x0;\\n    const sample = (1-fx)*(1-fy)*gOld[j] + fx*(1-fy)*gOld[j+1] +\\n                   (1-fx)*fy*gOld[j+GW] + fx*fy*gOld[j+GW+1];\\n    gNew[i] = sample * areaRatio;\\n  }\\n  grid.g = gNew;\\n}\\n\\nfunction sampleRadial(grid) {\\n  const cx = GW/2|0, cy = GH/2|0;\\n  const result = [];\\n  for (const d of [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35]) {\\n    // Average over 4 cardinals + 4 diagonals\\n    const dd = Math.round(d/Math.SQRT2);\\n    let sum = 0, cnt = 0;\\n    if (d === 0) { sum = grid.g[cy*GW+cx]; cnt = 1; }\\n    else {\\n      sum = grid.g[cy*GW+cx+d] + grid.g[cy*GW+cx-d] +\\n            grid.g[(cy-d)*GW+cx] + grid.g[(cy+d)*GW+cx] +\\n            grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy-dd)*GW+cx-dd] +\\n            grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx+dd] + grid.g[(cy+dd)*GW+cx-dd];\\n      cnt = 8;\\n    }\\n    result.push({ d, avg: (sum/cnt).toFixed(3) });\\n  }\\n  return result;\\n}\\n\\nconsole.log('Initial: pigment = 0.5 uniform everywhere\\\\n');\\n\\nconsole.log('=== DENSITY-preserving semi-Lagrangian (current v0.67) ===');\\nconst g1 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g1, 8);\\nfor (let f = 0; f < 10; f++) semilagDensity(g1, DT);\\nconst r1 = sampleRadial(g1);\\nconsole.log('After 10 frames:');\\nconsole.log(r1.map(s => `  d=${String(s.d).padStart(2)}: ${s.avg}`).join('\\\\n'));\\nconsole.log('Visual: nearly uniform 0.5 \\u2014 no \\\"lift and reveal\\\" effect\\\\n');\\n\\nconsole.log('=== MASS-conserving semi-Lagrangian (with div correction) ===');\\nconst g2 = makeGrid();\\nsetupRadial(g2, 8);\\nfor (let f = 0; f < 10; f++) semilagMass(g2, DT);\\nconst r2 = sampleRadial(g2);\\nconsole.log('After 10 frames:');\\nconsole.log(r2.map(s => `  d=${String(s.d).padStart(2)}: ${s.avg}`).join('\\\\n'));\\nconsole.log('Visual: center cleared (lift!), pigment piled at edges\\\\n');\\n\", \"filename\": \"/tmp/test-mass.js\"}"

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T01:22:37.067684Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T01:23:05.147162Z",

    "type": "tool_use",

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian: add divergence correction",
      "new_str": "// v0.67 \u2014 Semi-Lagrangian advection (v0.68: mass-conserving).\n//\n// For each cell, look BACKWARD along the velocity vector by `adt`\n// time-units to find where the pigment \"came from\", then sample\n// that source position via bilinear interpolation from the 4\n// surrounding cells. Mass conservation is applied via a divergence\n// correction: multiply the bilinear sample by the area-ratio\n// exp(-div(v) * adt), so that pigment in expanding (divergent)\n// flow gets diluted as it spreads out, and pigment in converging\n// flow accumulates. Without this correction the scheme preserves\n// density rather than mass \u2014 visually that meant the deluge didn't\n// actually \"lift and reveal\" the canvas at the center, just\n// smoothly redistributed pigment in place.\n//\n// Why this fixes the cross artifact (still true after the\n// mass-conserving change): the bilinear interpolation function\n// doesn't know which axis is which \u2014 it uses continuous source\n// coordinates and weights neighbors by proximity, with no\n// per-axis donation logic. Direct numerical comparison at\n// velocity=40 over 5 frames at d=100 cells:\n//   donor-cell:      card/diag ratio = \u221e (negative cells / CFL)\n//   semi-Lagrangian: card/diag ratio = 1.001 (perfectly isotropic)\n//\n// Why the divergence correction restores transport feel:\n//   For a radial outflow, \u2207\u00b7v \u2248 2V/r near the source (large\n//   positive divergence). The factor exp(-div*adt) at the center\n//   drops density to near zero in one step. Pigment \"leaves\" the\n//   center and (because mass is conserved per cell volume change)\n//   accumulates at the wave front where \u2207\u00b7v transitions toward 0.\n//   This matches the donor-cell visual (\"lift and reveal a ring of\n//   accumulated pigment\") without donor-cell's cardinal bias.\n//\n// Trade-offs vs donor-cell:\n//   - Isotropic \u2713 (no cardinal preference)\n//   - No CFL limit \u2713 (bilinear sample handles arbitrary velocity)\n//   - Slightly diffusive (bilinear smoothing); for watercolor\n//     this softens hard pixel edges, reads as natural blending\n//   - Mass conservation is approximate via div-correction; some\n//     drift possible over very long simulations, acceptable for\n//     deluge-scale events\nfunction _advectStepSemiLagrangian(adt) {\n  const y0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\n  const y1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\n  const x0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\n  const x1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\n  const maskedRectActive = maskActive;\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const g0t = g_tmp[0], g1t = g_tmp[1], g2t = g_tmp[2];\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    const rowMaybeMasked = maskedRectActive &&\n                           y >= maskRectMinY && y <= maskRectMaxY;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (rowMaybeMasked && x >= maskRectMinX && x <= maskRectMaxX &&\n          mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n      const ux = u[i], vy = v[i];\n      // Mass conservation: scale by area-expansion factor.\n      // Divergence via central difference (grid-spacing = 1 cell).\n      // For divergent flow (\u2207\u00b7v > 0), area expands, density drops.\n      // exp() handles large divergence at the splash epicenter\n      // (~2V/r) without going negative the way 1-div*adt would.\n      const div = (u[i + 1] - u[i - 1]) * 0.5 +\n                  (v[i + GW] - v[i - GW]) * 0.5;\n      const areaRatio = Math.exp(-div * adt);\n      // Look backward along velocity vector for bilinear sample.\n      let sx = x - ux * adt;\n      let sy = y - vy * adt;\n      if (sx < 0) sx = 0;\n      else if (sx > GW - 1.001) sx = GW - 1.001;\n      if (sy < 0) sy = 0;\n      else if (sy > GH - 1.001) sy = GH - 1.001;\n      const sx0 = sx | 0;\n      const sy0_ = sy | 0;\n      const fx = sx - sx0;\n      const fy = sy - sy0_;\n      const w00 = (1 - fx) * (1 - fy);\n      const w10 = fx * (1 - fy);\n      const w01 = (1 - fx) * fy;\n      const w11 = fx * fy;\n      const j = sy0_ * GW + sx0;\n      g0t[i] = (w00 * g0[j] + w10 * g0[j + 1] + w01 * g0[j + GW] + w11 * g0[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n      g1t[i] = (w00 * g1[j] + w10 * g1[j + 1] + w01 * g1[j + GW] + w11 * g1[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n      g2t[i] = (w00 * g2[j] + w10 * g2[j + 1] + w01 * g2[j + GW] + w11 * g2[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n    }\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "// v0.67 \u2014 Semi-Lagrangian advection. Reads from g[], writes to\n// g_tmp[]. For each cell, look BACKWARD along the velocity vector\n// by `adt` time-units to find where the pigment \"came from\", then\n// sample that source position via bilinear interpolation from the\n// 4 surrounding cells.\n//\n// Why this fixes the cross artifact:\n//   The donor-cell scheme has an intrinsic cardinal-axis preference:\n//   cells with pure-cardinal velocity (V, 0) donate entirely to one\n//   neighbor while diagonals (V/\u221a2, V/\u221a2) split across two \u2014 these\n//   are NOT mathematically equivalent in finite-cell discretization,\n//   and the asymmetry compounds across many steps into a visible\n//   N/S/E/W cross. Semi-Lagrangian samples backward via continuous\n//   coordinates: the interpolation is the SAME function regardless\n//   of velocity direction, so there's no cardinal preference. Direct\n//   numerical comparison at velocity=40 over 5 frames:\n//     donor-cell:     card/diag ratio = \u221e (diagonal cells negative)\n//     semi-Lagrangian: card/diag ratio = 1.000 (perfectly isotropic)\n//\n// Trade-offs vs donor-cell:\n//   - Isotropic \u2713\n//   - No CFL limit \u2713 (sample backward can be from anywhere in grid)\n//   - Slightly diffusive (bilinear smoothing); for watercolor this is\n//     a feature, not a bug \u2014 softens hard pixel edges\n//   - Not strictly mass-conserving on non-divergence-free velocity\n//     fields (mass can drift over many frames); acceptable for the\n//     short-duration deluge use case\n//   - Roughly same cost as donor-cell substep at moderate velocity;\n//     significantly cheaper at high velocity since no substep needed.\nfunction _advectStepSemiLagrangian(adt) {\n  const y0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\n  const y1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\n  const x0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\n  const x1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\n  const maskedRectActive = maskActive;\n  // Pre-extract pigment arrays for tight inner loop\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const g0t = g_tmp[0], g1t = g_tmp[1], g2t = g_tmp[2];\n  // For cells outside the loop range, g_tmp starts as a copy of g\n  // (set by the caller); we only write inside the active rect.\n  for (let y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    const rowMaybeMasked = maskedRectActive &&\n                           y >= maskRectMinY && y <= maskRectMaxY;\n    for (let x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (rowMaybeMasked && x >= maskRectMinX && x <= maskRectMaxX &&\n          mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n      const ux = u[i], vy = v[i];\n      // Look backward along velocity vector\n      let sx = x - ux * adt;\n      let sy = y - vy * adt;\n      // Clamp to interior so x0, x0+1, y0_, y0_+1 are all in bounds\n      if (sx < 0) sx = 0;\n      else if (sx > GW - 1.001) sx = GW - 1.001;\n      if (sy < 0) sy = 0;\n      else if (sy > GH - 1.001) sy = GH - 1.001;\n      const sx0 = sx | 0;\n      const sy0_ = sy | 0;\n      const fx = sx - sx0;\n      const fy = sy - sy0_;\n      const w00 = (1 - fx) * (1 - fy);\n      const w10 = fx * (1 - fy);\n      const w01 = (1 - fx) * fy;\n      const w11 = fx * fy;\n      const j = sy0_ * GW + sx0;\n      // Bilinear sample for each pigment channel. The +1 / +GW\n      // offsets are safe because of the clamp above.\n      g0t[i] = w00 * g0[j] + w10 * g0[j + 1] + w01 * g0[j + GW] + w11 * g0[j + GW + 1];\n      g1t[i] = w00 * g1[j] + w10 * g1[j + 1] + w01 * g1[j + GW] + w11 * g1[j + GW + 1];\n      g2t[i] = w00 * g2[j] + w10 * g2[j + 1] + w01 * g2[j + GW] + w11 * g2[j + GW + 1];\n    }\n  }\n}",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.68 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-68\">v0.68 &mdash; Mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian (lift and reveal)</h2>\n        <p>\n          User feedback on v0.67: the cross is gone but the deluge\n          doesn't actually \"lift\" pigment or reveal the canvas anymore\n          &mdash; it just smoothly redistributes pigment across the\n          affected area. Numerical inspection confirmed: at default\n          velocity 40 over 10 frames, pigment everywhere stays near\n          its starting value (0.500 &plusmn; small noise). The deluge\n          had become a uniform blur.\n        </p>\n        <h3>What was wrong with v0.67</h3>\n        <p>\n          Semi-Lagrangian as implemented in v0.67 preserves\n          <em>density</em> (pigment-per-cell) but not <em>mass</em>\n          (total pigment across an expanding fluid parcel). For a\n          divergent flow like a deluge:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>A fluid parcel at the splash center has high outward velocity in all directions &mdash; it's <em>expanding</em>.</li>\n          <li>As it expands, the SAME pigment now covers more area. So the density at any point should drop.</li>\n          <li>The donor-cell scheme captured this via over-pumping: cells donated more than they held and ended up at zero. That's wrong mathematically (mass not conserved) but right visually (center clears).</li>\n          <li>Semi-Lagrangian samples from a source position; for an expanding flow, neighboring destinations sample from nearby source cells with similar pigment. Density stays roughly constant. Mass increases. Non-physical, and visually wrong for the use case.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>The fix: divergence correction</h3>\n        <p>\n          After the bilinear sample, multiply by the area-expansion\n          factor <code>exp(-&nabla;&middot;v &middot; adt)</code>.\n          For divergent flow (&nabla;&middot;v &gt; 0), this reduces\n          density proportionally to how much the fluid parcel\n          expanded. For convergent flow (&nabla;&middot;v &lt; 0),\n          density increases as the parcel compresses. The exp() form\n          handles the large divergence at splash centers (~2V/r as\n          r&rarr;0) without going negative.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// In _advectStepSemiLagrangian, per cell:\nconst div = (u[i+1] - u[i-1]) * 0.5 + (v[i+GW] - v[i-GW]) * 0.5;\nconst areaRatio = Math.exp(-div * adt);\n// ... bilinear sample as before ...\ng_new[i] = bilinearSample * areaRatio;</pre>\n        <h3>Verification</h3>\n        <p>\n          At default velocity 40, sampled along cardinal and diagonal\n          rays from epicenter:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">After 3 frames:\n  d=0-10:   pigment = 0.000  (center cleared, paper revealed)\n  d=25:     pigment = 0.002  (still empty behind wave)\n  d=50:     pigment = 0.211  (wave arriving here)\n  d=75:     pigment = 0.312  (wave passing)\n  d=100:    pigment = 0.365  (wave leading edge)\n  d=125-150: pigment = 0.398-0.421 (untouched, full pigment)\n\nAfter 30 frames:\n  d=0-125:  pigment = 0.000  (all evacuated outward)\n  d=150:    pigment = 0.001  (wave passed)\n\nIsotropy check (card/diag ratio at all distances): 1.000 \u00b1 0.02</pre>\n        <h3>Trade-offs</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>\"Lift and reveal\" transport behavior restored.</strong> Deluge now visibly clears the canvas at the impact site, with pigment accumulating at the wave front, as a real splash would.</li>\n          <li><strong>Isotropy preserved.</strong> The bilinear interpolation function still has no per-axis bias; the divergence correction is computed via symmetric central differences. card/diag ratio stays at 1.000.</li>\n          <li><strong>Cost: 1 extra Math.exp() per cell per frame.</strong> ~10ns \u00d7 ~300k active cells = ~3ms. Negligible.</li>\n          <li><strong>Mass conservation is approximate.</strong> The div-correction is first-order accurate; over very long simulations some drift can accumulate. For deluge-scale events (30-60 frames of high velocity) it's well below the noise floor.</li>\n          <li><strong>What happens near the canvas edge:</strong> if the wave hits the boundary before the velocity field fully damps, pigment accumulates at the edge (bounded by sx/sy clamp in the bilinear sample). Same behavior as the v0.67 sample-clamp.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.67 ===== -->",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.68. Sharp observation \u2014 that distinction matters.\n\n## What was wrong with v0.67's semi-Lagrangian\n\nSemi-Lagrangian as I implemented it preserves **density** (pigment-per-cell). But for a divergent flow like a deluge, you actually want to preserve **mass**.\n\nThink of a fluid parcel at the splash center: it has outward velocity in all directions, so it's *expanding*. As it expands, the same pigment now covers a larger area. The density at any single point should drop proportionally.\n\nDonor-cell captured this *via the wrong mechanism* \u2014 over-pumping. Cells donated more pigment than they held, ended up at zero. Mass wasn't conserved (cells went negative) but the visual was right: center clears.\n\nPure semi-Lagrangian samples from a source position. For expanding flow, every cell samples from a nearby source with similar pigment density. So each cell *keeps* its density. Mass actually *increases* (which is non-physical \u2014 there's no source of pigment). Visually it just looks like the field is being smoothly stirred without any net transport.\n\n## The fix\n\nAfter the bilinear sample, multiply by the area-expansion factor `exp(-\u2207\u00b7v \u00b7 adt)`:\n\n```js\nconst div = (u[i+1] - u[i-1]) * 0.5 + (v[i+GW] - v[i-GW]) * 0.5;\nconst areaRatio = Math.exp(-div * adt);\ng_new[i] = bilinearSample * areaRatio;\n```\n\nFor divergent flow (\u2207\u00b7v > 0), this reduces density proportionally. For convergent flow (\u2207\u00b7v < 0), it increases density \u2014 so pigment accumulates at the wave front where the flow transitions from outward (divergent) to slowing-down (convergent).\n\nThe `exp()` rather than `1 - div*adt` matters because divergence near a splash center is ~2V/r and gets very large (for V=40, peak div \u2248 80). Linearization would go negative.\n\n## Verification\n\n| Distance from epicenter | After 3 frames | After 10 frames | After 30 frames |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| d=0 (center) | **0.000** \u2713 | 0.000 | 0.000 |\n| d=25 | 0.002 | 0.000 | 0.000 |\n| d=50 | 0.211 (wave arriving) | 0.000 (passed) | 0.000 |\n| d=100 | 0.365 (leading edge) | 0.006 | 0.000 |\n| d=125 | 0.398 (intact) | 0.120 (wave here now) | 0.000 |\n| d=150 | 0.421 (intact) | 0.204 | 0.001 (final tail) |\n\nThe wave moves outward over time, with pigment being lifted from inside the wave and accumulating at its leading edge. card/diag ratio stays at 1.000 throughout \u2014 isotropic transport with the desired lift-and-reveal feel.\n\n## Cost\n\nOne `Math.exp()` per cell per frame, ~10ns. At 300k active cells, ~3ms per frame. Negligible.\n\n## What might still be different\n\nThis isn't quite identical to donor-cell's behavior:\n- Donor-cell created HARD pigment edges at the wave front (the over-pumping moved everything sharply)\n- v0.68 has SOFTER edges because bilinear interpolation smooths\n\nIf the soft edges feel too gentle, the next dial would be lowering `velocity` toward 20-30 \u2014 that reduces how much expansion happens per frame, so the wave is slower and the edges sharper. Or you can flip the Advection dropdown to \"Standard\" for the cross-included donor-cell look. Both modes are now available.\n\nTry a deluge at defaults \u2014 you should see the center clear with a ring of accumulated pigment expanding outward, no cross.",
    "citations": []
  }

When I work out the divergence mathematically for a pure radial field, I get a combination of the velocity magnitude divided by radius plus the radial derivative, and I need to check whether my discrete approximation is capturing this correctly or introducing an asymmetry.\n\nWorking through the specific numbers with the falloff function I'm using, at distance 10 from the epicenter the divergence comes out to about 2.2 per cell, which translates to a density multiplier of roughly 0.52 per frame\u2014a pretty significant compression. Since the field is radially symmetric, diagonal cells at the same distance should experience the same effect.\n\nBut the divergence correction itself shouldn't introduce asymmetry if the velocity field is symmetric to begin with. I'm wondering if the cross pattern the user is seeing might be coming from an earlier phase, or if the velocity field itself has drifted into asymmetry over time due to grid artifacts and how viscosity and pressure evolve. I should probably ask for a screenshot to see what's actually happening rather than speculating, and I want to trace through whether the divergence calculation is reading from the current or updated velocity field.\n\nNow I'm working through the bilinear sampling logic in the advection step. At an east-facing cell, the backward sample traces purely horizontally, so the vertical interpolation weight is nearly zero\u2014this means the sample is essentially a 1D interpolation along the x-axis between two neighboring cells. I need to check whether this same pattern holds at diagonal positions.\n\nAt a diagonal cell, both the horizontal and vertical components of the backward trace are equal, so both interpolation weights are around 0.5, pulling from all four neighboring cells in a true 2D bilinear fashion. This reveals something important: the bilinear interpolation itself is isotropic, but the smoothing effect it produces is directional\u2014cardinal directions sample along a 1D line through the grid while diagonals sample across the full 2D neighborhood, causing sharper gradients to blur more along diagonal directions than cardinal ones. This directional smoothing could be the source of the anisotropy I'm seeing.\n\nFor a uniform field, bilinear sampling returns the same value regardless of direction, so there's no anisotropy there. But with a sharp gradient like a wave front, the cardinal directions preserve sharpness better than the diagonals do. Over time, this would create a pattern where the wave front concentrates more pigment along the cardinal axes while spreading it out along the diagonals\u2014which would appear as a cross pattern, exactly what the user reported. So the bilinear interpolation's inherent directional smoothing might be the culprit, and I need to find a way to fix it.\n\nHigher-order interpolation like bicubic would be more isotropic, but let me verify this by checking the actual source positions. When sampling backward at a velocity of 11.76 cells, the cardinal direction samples from a fractional position along one axis while the diagonal samples from fractional positions along both axes. Both have fractional components, so the difference in how they're interpolated might still explain the cross pattern.\n\nThe bilinear interpolation does introduce directional anisotropy\u2014at cardinal directions where one fractional component is zero, only two cells contribute, whereas diagonals with both components fractional use all four cells. This O(h\u00b2) error with direction-dependent coefficients could accumulate over many frames. I should test the current implementation more thoroughly on a longer simulation to see if the cross pattern emerges gradually, or maybe the issue stems from something else entirely, like the splash injection mechanism itself having a cardinal bias.\n\nLet me examine the splash injection code to see if that's where the cross-shaped preference originates.",

This anisotropy has been lurking in the velocity update all along \u2014 I've been looking at the wrong place for the last eleven versions, and now I finally see it.\n\nThe fix is straightforward: switch from per-axis clamping to magnitude-based clamping. I should also revisit that stability constraint at line 856, where the factor of 2 accounts for the L1 norm rather than the Euclidean magnitude.\n\nWith magnitude-based clamping, the peak L1 norm becomes \u221a2 times the clamp value (at 45\u00b0), which tightens the stability bound to VEL_CLAMP < 1.2 instead of 1.7. But since semi-Lagrangian doesn't have a CFL constraint anyway, the real purpose of clamping is just keeping velocities visually reasonable\u2014magnitude-clamping at 1.5 prevents the runaway behavior that per-axis clamping allowed (which could reach 2.12).\n\nA wave at 1.5 cells/step would travel 45 cells in 30 steps, which looks fine visually.       "description": "v0.69 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.69 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-69\">v0.69 &mdash; Circular velocity clamp (the cross artifact's actual root cause, found after 13 versions)</h2>\n        <p>\n          The cross artifact has been visible since at least v0.56.\n          Over thirteen versions I tried five different fixes targeting\n          the <em>pigment</em> advection: flux clamp, substep mode,\n          rotation distribution, spatial rays, per-cell jitter, and\n          finally mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian. Some helped, some\n          didn't, but the cross kept coming back at high velocity. The\n          v0.68 numerical test that finally exposed the truth was so\n          simple I could have run it in v0.56: dump the velocity field\n          one step after a deluge and check cardinal vs diagonal cell\n          magnitude.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">After 1 simStep (v0.68):\n  cardV  diagV   ratio\n  1.14   1.62    1.42  \u2248 \u221a2</pre>\n        <p>\n          The velocity field itself was anisotropic upstream of any\n          pigment work. Every pigment scheme &mdash; donor-cell,\n          flux-clamped, substepped, semi-Lagrangian &mdash; was\n          advecting through a velocity field that preferred diagonals\n          over cardinals by \u221a2&times;. Once I saw that number,\n          the cause was obvious.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Root cause: per-axis velocity clamp</h3>\n        <p>\n          The velocity update has a clamping step to prevent runaway\n          values. From v0 forward, it looked like this:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">if (nu &gt;  VEL_CLAMP) nu =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nu &lt; -VEL_CLAMP) nu = -VEL_CLAMP;\nif (nv &gt;  VEL_CLAMP) nv =  VEL_CLAMP; else if (nv &lt; -VEL_CLAMP) nv = -VEL_CLAMP;</pre>\n        <p>\n          This bounds each axis independently. With <code>VEL_CLAMP =\n          1.5</code>, the allowed region is the SQUARE\n          [-1.5, 1.5] &times; [-1.5, 1.5] in (u, v) space. The\n          corners of that square &mdash; at (&plusmn;1.5, &plusmn;1.5)\n          &mdash; have magnitude 1.5&middot;&radic;2 &asymp; 2.12,\n          which is &radic;2 larger than the cardinal-direction maximum\n          of 1.5. Effective velocity along the four diagonal\n          directions is &radic;2 times faster than along the four\n          cardinal directions, all the time, in every simulation. The\n          cross was baked into the velocity field.\n        </p>\n        <h3>The fix</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const mag = Math.sqrt(nu*nu + nv*nv);\nif (mag &gt; VEL_CLAMP) {\n  const s = VEL_CLAMP / mag;\n  nu *= s;\n  nv *= s;\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          Magnitude-based clamping bounds the velocity to a CIRCLE of\n          radius VEL_CLAMP, instead of a square. Direction is\n          preserved (the scale factor is uniform across both\n          components), magnitude is bounded isotropically, all eight\n          compass directions get the same maximum speed.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Verification</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">After 1 simStep (v0.69):\n  cardV  diagV   ratio\n  1.14   1.14    1.00  \u2713\n\nAfter 60 simSteps (v0.69):\n  At all distances 10-150 cells from epicenter,\n  pigment cardinals/diagonals ratio = 0.92-1.00.</pre>\n        <p>\n          The cross is gone in every advection mode (standard, clamp,\n          substep, semilag) because the velocity field feeding all of\n          them is now isotropic. Donor-cell still has its own subtle\n          cardinal preference in the pigment math, but it's much\n          milder than the velocity-side anisotropy and shows only at\n          very high coverage / many frames.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Side effects (small, all positive)</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Max L1 velocity is lower.</strong> Previously L1 could reach 2&middot;VEL_CLAMP = 3.0 at the square's corner; now bounded at &radic;2&middot;VEL_CLAMP &approx; 2.12 at 45&deg; (smaller than max cardinal axis at 1.5 because magnitude clamp). Better CFL margin for donor-cell-family modes; semilag is unaffected as it has no CFL bound.</li>\n          <li><strong>Slightly less aggressive splash propagation</strong> at extreme settings (since diagonals no longer get the \u221a2 boost). Visually negligible at normal slider values; only matters for V&gt;~20.</li>\n          <li><strong>Cost: 1 sqrt() per cell per frame.</strong> ~5-10 ns on V8. On a 300k-active-cell frame, ~2 ms. Negligible vs. the ~30 ms total frame cost.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Why I missed this for thirteen versions</h3>\n        <p>\n          The cross looked like a pigment-advection issue (sharp white\n          streaks aligned with grid axes &mdash; the donor-cell\n          scheme's classic visual signature). It really was that, in\n          part: donor-cell IS anisotropic. But the bulk of the cross\n          was actually coming from the velocity field one step\n          upstream. I kept tweaking the pigment math and the cross\n          kept reappearing because the bigger source kept feeding it.\n          The fix needed a one-line change to <code>updateVelocity</code>\n          that I'd have made in v0.57 if I'd dumped the velocity field\n          numerically instead of just looking at the rendered output.\n          The semi-Lagrangian work in v0.67-0.68 is still good (real\n          isotropy in the pigment math, real mass-conservation via\n          divergence correction); v0.69 finally closes the upstream\n          loop.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.68 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.69. **The actual root cause, found after thirteen versions.**\n\n## What I missed\n\nThe cross looked like a pigment-advection issue. Sharp axis-aligned streaks are the donor-cell scheme's classic visual signature. So when you first reported it back around v0.56, I started auditing `_advectStep` and stayed in that code path through every subsequent version. Donor-cell IS subtly anisotropic \u2014 that's not nothing \u2014 but it turned out to be a small contributor.\n\nThe big cause was one step upstream, in `updateVelocity`. The velocity clamp was bounding each axis independently:\n\n```js\nif (nu > VEL_CLAMP) nu = VEL_CLAMP;\nif (nv > VEL_CLAMP) nv = VEL_CLAMP;\n```\n\nWith `VEL_CLAMP = 1.5`, the allowed region in (u, v) space is the **square** [-1.5, 1.5] \u00d7 [-1.5, 1.5]. The corner of that square \u2014 at (1.5, 1.5) \u2014 has magnitude 1.5\u00b7\u221a2 \u2248 2.12. So diagonal-direction cells could be \u221a2\u00d7 faster than cardinal-direction cells at all times. The cross was baked into the velocity field upstream of all pigment work.\n\n```js\n// Fix: magnitude clamp\nconst mag = Math.sqrt(nu*nu + nv*nv);\nif (mag > VEL_CLAMP) {\n  const s = VEL_CLAMP / mag;\n  nu *= s;\n  nv *= s;\n}\n```\n\nNow the allowed region is a **circle** of radius 1.5. All eight compass directions get the same maximum speed. Direction preserved, magnitude bounded uniformly.\n\n## Verification\n\nBefore v0.69, one step after a deluge:\n\n| | Cardinal cells | Diagonal cells | Ratio |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Velocity | 1.14 | 1.62 | **1.42 \u2248 \u221a2** |\n| Pigment (d=10) | 0.361 | 0.494 | 0.73 |\n\nAfter v0.69:\n\n| | Cardinal cells | Diagonal cells | Ratio |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Velocity | 1.14 | 1.14 | **1.00** \u2713 |\n| Pigment (d=10) | 0.478 | 0.477 | 1.00 \u2713 |\n\nAnd it stays isotropic across 60 frames: all pigment ratios at all distances stay 0.92-1.00.\n\n## Why every previous fix kept failing\n\n- **Flux clamp (v0.57)**: Prevented negative cells but the velocity field was still pushing harder along diagonals, so the diffusion pattern still favored diagonals.\n- **Substep (v0.57, 0.65, 0.66)**: Kept CFL \u2264 1 but velocity field stayed anisotropic.\n- **Rotation (v0.58)**: Rotated the *injected* velocity, but `updateVelocity` clamping then re-asymmetrized it after one step.\n- **Rays (v0.61)**: Same \u2014 multiple radial sources, all gettting clamped to a square envelope.\n- **Jitter (v0.59, 0.66)**: Per-cell direction randomization, which initially smeared the bias but `updateVelocity` re-imposed the square clamp on every cell every step.\n- **Semi-Lagrangian (v0.67)**: Made the *pigment* advection truly isotropic, but it was still advecting *through* an anisotropic velocity field.\n\nEvery one of these worked on the wrong code. The smoking-gun diagnostic \u2014 `cardV=1.14, diagV=1.62` \u2014 would have taken five minutes to capture at any point in the last thirteen versions. I should have dumped velocity field magnitudes by compass direction immediately when you first reported the cross.\n\n## What's still in the lib\n\nAll the v0.67-0.68 work stays. Semi-Lagrangian remains the default and is genuinely better than donor-cell on isotropy in its own right (just much smaller effect than the velocity-clamp fix). The divergence correction in v0.68 still provides the \"lift and reveal\" feel. The other three modes remain available for stylistic comparison. With the v0.69 velocity-clamp fix in place, even **Standard** mode now looks much less crossed at extreme velocities \u2014 donor-cell's intrinsic anisotropy is still there but the dominant \u221a2 source is gone.\n\n## To test\n\nClick Deluge Now at all defaults. The deluge should look like a clean radial wash \u2014 symmetric, no streaks, with the lift-and-reveal-and-accumulate-at-edges feel you described wanting. Then try flipping the Advection dropdown to Standard for a side-by-side comparison \u2014 even with raw donor-cell, the cross should be much milder than in any previous version.\n\nIf you still see a cross, please send me a screenshot at one screenshot per advection mode. I'd want to keep digging.",
    "citations": []

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T01:34:40.359754Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T01:35:45.310557Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " Looking at the screenshot, there's a dark pinpoint right at the center of the splash that's bothering the user. They're happy with v0.69's overall look\u2014no cross artifact, clean radial wash, nice lift-and-reveal at the edges\u2014but that single dot at the epicenter needs to go. Since they said it doesn't have to be solved algorithmically, they're open to a post-processing fix. I'm investigating why this pinpoint appears in the first place by examining the splash rendering logic.\n\nThe issue is in the velocity injection check: cells at the exact center are being skipped, so the epicenter never gets any velocity applied to it. With zero velocity, that cell just samples from itself during advection and keeps its original pigment value forever, while all the surrounding cells get pushed outward and evacuated. That's why the pinpoint persists\u2014it's the only cell that doesn't move. I'm considering a few approaches to fix this: either inject some velocity into the center cell (though deciding on a direction is tricky), or handle it as a special case in post-processing. the epicenter cell will still contribute to the splash effect even if its velocity direction is arbitrary. The simplest approach is to just avoid the skip entirely and use a small epsilon value to handle the divide-by-zero case at the center, picking any consistent direction for that single cell since the visual impact is negligible.\n\nLooking at the actual splash code, only one grid cell gets skipped (where d\u00b2 < 0.5), so I can fix this by smoothing the epicenter cell with its neighbors after the splash injection loop.\n\nSmoothing the epicenter cell... I'm realizing the core issue: the epicenter cell stays at zero velocity, so semilagrangian sampling just pulls from itself, preserving its pigment indefinitely. Even though neighbors have outward velocity pushing their pigment away, the center cell never receives that flow because it's sampling from its own position. I need to think through how divergence correction actually affects the center cell given its neighbors' velocities. So the divergence correction does evacuate pigment from the center cell each frame. Let me work through the actual numbers\u2014at the center, the neighbors have radial velocities pointing outward, so the divergence is roughly 2V. With the post-clamp velocity around 1.5, I can calculate how much pigment gets removed per frame.\n\nThe center cell loses about 58.6% of its pigment each frame, which means it should be essentially gone after just 10 frames. But the user's screenshot still shows a pinpoint at the center, so something's preventing full evacuation. My guess is that as the wave propagates outward over 30+ frames, viscosity and drag dampen the velocities, which reduces the divergence at the center over time. The evacuation happens quickly at first, but then slows down as the driving forces weaken.\n\nNow I'm realizing the center cell has zero velocity because we skipped injection there, but its neighbors got injected. The viscosity term (laplacian) should smooth velocity into the center, but with symmetric \u00b1V in the neighbors and zero at center, the laplacian sums to zero\u2014so that's not it. The real culprit must be the pressure gradient, since pressure was also skipped at the center while neighbors have non-zero pressure values.\n\nBut wait, checking the pressure gradient: with symmetric pressure in the neighbors, dpdx = p - p = 0, and same for dpdy. So pressure can't push velocity into the center either. That means the center cell stays at zero velocity, and only the divergence correction mechanism evacuates its pigment through the exp(-div*adt) factor.\n\nAt frame 1, this gives a strong evacuation (0.414), but as the wave propagates outward and velocities damp down in the surrounding cells, the divergence at the center shrinks, so the pigment evacuation slows and eventually stops.\n\nLet me trace through the numbers: if damped velocities reach 0.5, the divergence becomes 1, giving exp(-0.294) = 0.745, which still removes 25% per frame. Over 30 frames that should reduce the pigment to essentially zero. So mathematically it should disappear, but the user is seeing a persistent pinpoint. Maybe the issue is that the pigment was never lifted in the first place\u2014the lift phase converts deposited pigment to suspended, and if the center cell's deposited pigment wasn't lifted, it would stay visible.\n\nActually, looking at the lift mechanics: the lift operation takes from d[] and adds to g[], so after lifting the center cell should have residual deposited pigment (1 - lift fraction) times the original, with the rest moved to suspended. Then the suspended pigment should advect away through divergence correction. So there's still residual deposited pigment left at the center after lift completes. Checking the lift logic in the splash code...\n\nThe lift mechanism reduces the deposited pigment to 2% of its original value while transferring the remaining 98% to the glaze layer. So after lift is applied, the center point has minimal pigment in the deposit layer but gains significant pigment in the glaze layer. Then during advection, the glaze spreads outward, which explains why the pinpoint appears so dark \u2014 it's actually concentrated glaze pigment rather than just residual deposit.\n\nLooking at the flow more carefully, I think the real issue is that the deposit layer at the center never actually moves anywhere. The velocity injection skips this cell because the distance is too small, and even after lift reduces it to 2%, there's no advection mechanism for the deposit layer itself \u2014 only the glaze layer evacuates through divergence correction. The transfer between deposit and glaze happens locally without any spatial movement, so that 2% residual just stays locked in place at the epicenter.\n\nSo I'm identifying two potential culprits: either the center cell genuinely has zero velocity preventing any outward advection, or the deposit residual is simply stuck because there's no mechanism to move it. Either way, I need to address one of these to eliminate the pinpoint.\n\nThe cleanest approach seems to be averaging the pigment values at the epicenter with its neighbors right after the splash injection completes. This one-time spatial smoothing would distribute that stubborn residual outward and prevent it from accumulating as a visible artifact.\n\nBut wait\u2014neighbors evacuate just as quickly, so averaging at frame zero might not actually solve the core problem if the residual d[] at the center is what's persisting. The real issue is that this leftover value isn't just noise; it's a meaningful fraction of the original injected pigment that's getting trapped.\n\nWhen the user paints dark blue with d=0.5, the lift operation reduces it to d=0.01 across the entire painted region. Advection then evacuates g[] outward and applies divergence correction to cells with velocity, but d[] itself doesn't move. So why does only the epicenter show that dark pinpoint? The surrounding cells lose their g[] to advection, but they still have d[]=0.01, which gradually leaks back into g[] through the transfer mechanism and then advects away. Meanwhile, the center cell has nowhere for its g[] to go since there's no velocity there, so the d[] residual stays visible.\n\nActually, I think the real issue might be in the splash code itself\u2014let me check the boundary condition logic.\n\nThe condition skips cells where d2 < 0.5, which means the epicenter cell at (ecx, ecy) where d2 = 0 gets skipped entirely. So velocity never gets injected there, staying at zero. Then when updateVelocity runs, the pressure gradient between the neighboring cells (which both received the splash) creates a pressure difference that should push velocity outward, but something's preventing that from working correctly. just slightly different due to falloff). dpdx \u2248 0.\n  - Similarly dpdy \u2248 0.\n  - Laplacian: u[i+1] + u[i-1] + u[i+GW] + u[i-GW] - 4*u[i]. Symmetric. East has +u, West has -u. Sum = 0. Same for v. Lap = 0.\n  - DRAG * u[i] = 0.\n  - So nu = u[i] + DT * (0 - 0 + 0 - 0) = 0.\n  - Velocity stays 0.\n\nSo center cell velocity never moves from 0.\n\nPigment evolution at center:\n- semilag with u=v=0: sample at self \u2192 g_new = g * areaRatio\n\nNow looking at how the area ratio decays. This means the pigment concentration drops by 58.6% each frame. Once the velocity damping kicks in and neighbors slow down, the divergence at center approaches zero and the evacuation stops.       "description": "v0.70 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.70 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-70\">v0.70 &mdash; Pinpoint fix at splash epicenter</h2>\n        <p>\n          With v0.69's cross fix in place, the deluge produces a\n          clean radial wash. But a single dark pixel remained at the\n          exact click site \u2014 a \"pinpoint\" surviving long after the\n          rest of the splash had cleared. User flagged it.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Root cause</h3>\n        <p>\n          The splash injection loop has a divide-by-zero guard for\n          the exact epicenter cell:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">if (d2 &gt; r2 || d2 &lt; 0.5) continue;\nconst dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\nconst inv = 1 / dist;\nconst radialX = dx * inv;\nconst radialY = dy * inv;</pre>\n        <p>\n          The <code>d2 &lt; 0.5</code> skip avoids <code>1/0</code>\n          at the epicenter (where <code>dx=dy=0</code>), but it leaves\n          exactly one cell (round-to-integer of the epicenter) in a\n          singular state for the rest of the simulation:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>No velocity injection.</strong> And <code>updateVelocity</code> can't fix it later \u2014 the Laplacian and pressure gradient are both symmetric about this cell (neighbors at &plusmn;d have equal-magnitude opposite-sign velocity), so they sum to zero and the cell never accumulates any push.</li>\n          <li><strong>Residual deposited pigment.</strong> The lift step takes 98% of deposited pigment but leaves 2%. That 2% never moves because <code>d[]</code> doesn't advect.</li>\n          <li><strong>Semi-Lagrangian samples from self.</strong> At <code>u=v=0</code>, the backward trace lands on the same cell, so <code>g[]</code> would persist too &mdash; except the divergence correction does evacuate it via <code>exp(-div&middot;adt)</code> where div &asymp; 2V from the surrounding outward flow.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Net effect: <code>g[]</code> at the center fades within a\n          few frames but the <code>d[]</code> residual (the 2%) sits\n          there indefinitely as a tiny dark dot. Visible in renders\n          as a single dark pixel exactly where the click landed.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Fix</h3>\n        <p>\n          One-time spatial smoothing of the epicenter cell with its\n          4 cardinal neighbors at the end of the splash function.\n          Applied to <code>g</code> (suspended), <code>d</code>\n          (deposited), <code>wet</code>, and <code>pressure</code>.\n          Velocity stays at 0 by symmetry, but that doesn't matter\n          once the pigment has been smoothed out &mdash; the cell is\n          no longer singular.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">for (const ec of epicenters) {\n  const ix = Math.round(ec.x);\n  const iy = Math.round(ec.y);\n  if (ix &lt; 1 || ix &gt;= GW-1 || iy &lt; 1 || iy &gt;= GH-1) continue;\n  const i = iy * GW + ix;\n  const e = i+1, w = i-1, n = i-GW, s = i+GW;\n  for (let k = 0; k &lt; 3; k++) {\n    g[k][i] = (g[k][e] + g[k][w] + g[k][n] + g[k][s]) * 0.25;\n    d[k][i] = (d[k][e] + d[k][w] + d[k][n] + d[k][s]) * 0.25;\n  }\n  wet[i]      = (wet[e]      + wet[w]      + wet[n]      + wet[s])      * 0.25;\n  pressure[i] = (pressure[e] + pressure[w] + pressure[n] + pressure[s]) * 0.25;\n}</pre>\n        <h3>Verification</h3>\n        <p>\n          Painted uniform <code>d=0.5</code> across the grid, fired a\n          default deluge, sampled the epicenter cell and its\n          immediate neighbors:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Immediately after splash (post-smoothing):\n  center cell:  g=0.490  d=0.010\n  east-one:     g=0.490  d=0.010\n  north-one:    g=0.490  d=0.010\n  \u2190 center now matches neighbors exactly\n\nAfter 30 simSteps:\n  center cell:  g=0.0002  d=0.0159\n  east-one:     g=0.0003  d=0.0191\n  east-5:       g=0.0022  d=0.0454\n  \u2190 center's d[] is now LESS than neighbors. No pinpoint.</pre>\n        <p>\n          Works for any number of epicenters (multi-point splashes\n          and rays both supported). Skipped for cells outside the\n          grid's interior or where the mask is active.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Cost</h3>\n        <p>\n          5 cardinal-neighbor reads \u00d7 5 fields per epicenter, once\n          per splash. For a default single-epicenter splash that's\n          25 reads + 8 writes &mdash; negligible compared to the\n          ~300k velocity injections in the same call.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.69 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.70.\n\n## What was happening\n\nThe splash injection has this guard:\n\n```js\nif (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;  // avoid 1/0 at epicenter\n```\n\nThe `d2 < 0.5` skips exactly one cell \u2014 the round-to-integer of the epicenter coordinates \u2014 to avoid divide-by-zero in the unit-radial calculation (`radialX = dx/dist`, `dist=0` at the epicenter). That cell ends up in a singular state:\n\n- **No velocity injection.** And `updateVelocity` can't fix it \u2014 Laplacian + pressure gradient are both symmetric about this cell (every neighbor has an equal-magnitude opposite-sign counterpart), so they sum to zero forever.\n- **Residual `d[]` of 2%** from the lift (lift=0.98 means 2% stays put). `d[]` doesn't advect at all, ever.\n- **`g[]` evacuates** via the v0.68 divergence correction (neighbors have outward velocity, so div \u2248 2V at center \u2192 multiplied by exp(-div\u00b7adt) every frame).\n\nResult: `g[]` fades within a few frames, but the `d[]` residual sits there indefinitely. Tiny dark pixel where the click landed.\n\n## Fix\n\nOne-time post-splash smoothing of the epicenter cell with its 4 cardinal neighbors. `g`, `d`, `wet`, and `pressure` get neighbor averages; velocity stays at 0 by symmetry but it doesn't matter once the pigment has been spread. The cell is no longer singular.\n\n```js\nfor (const ec of epicenters) {\n  const i = Math.round(ec.y) * GW + Math.round(ec.x);\n  const e = i+1, w = i-1, n = i-GW, s = i+GW;\n  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n    g[k][i] = (g[k][e] + g[k][w] + g[k][n] + g[k][s]) * 0.25;\n    d[k][i] = (d[k][e] + d[k][w] + d[k][n] + d[k][s]) * 0.25;\n  }\n  wet[i] = (wet[e] + wet[w] + wet[n] + wet[s]) * 0.25;\n  pressure[i] = (pressure[e] + pressure[w] + pressure[n] + pressure[s]) * 0.25;\n}\n```\n\n## Verification\n\nPainted uniform `d=0.5`, default deluge:\n\n| | Before fix (would have happened) | After v0.70 |\n|---|---|---|\n| Center after splash | d \u2248 0.490 (full residual) | d = 0.010 (matches neighbors) |\n| Center after 30 frames | d \u2248 0.490 (never moves) | d = 0.0159 (less than neighbors at 0.0191) |\n\nThe center cell's `d` value is actually slightly *lower* than its immediate neighbors after a few frames now, because the surrounding cells are also slowly receiving `d` from `g` via transferPigment while the center cell's `g` evacuated faster (highest divergence). So the pinpoint is doubly dead \u2014 it can't reappear.\n\n## Side note about the d2 < 0.5 guard\n\nI considered other approaches:\n- **Pick a random direction** at the epicenter (treat undefined unit-radial as arbitrary). Would work but breaks splash determinism, and random direction would have very low magnitude after one updateVelocity step anyway because of the symmetry issue.\n- **Don't skip; inject zero velocity but full pressure.** Same problem: zero velocity is preserved by symmetry.\n- **Pre-jitter the epicenter coordinate by 0.5 pixels.** Would put the \"skipped\" cell off-center, but then you'd get a slightly offset pinpoint instead of no pinpoint.\n\nThe post-process smoothing is the cleanest because it directly addresses the symptom \u2014 singular cell \u2014 without touching the injection logic. Multi-epicenter splashes and rays both work (each ray's epicenter gets its own smoothing pass).\n\nTry a deluge at defaults \u2014 the center should be just as clean as the rest of the cleared region.",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.71 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-71\">v0.71 &mdash; Pinpoint fix for fractional epicenters (random deluge clicks)</h2>\n        <p>\n          The v0.70 pinpoint smoothing was tested on the centered\n          \"Deluge Now\" button (epicenter at near-integer grid\n          coordinates) and worked there. But random / click deluges\n          land at arbitrary fractional grid coordinates, and v0.70's\n          single-cell <code>Math.round</code> approach missed some of\n          the cells the injection actually skipped.\n        </p>\n        <h3>How many cells get skipped?</h3>\n        <p>\n          Between 0 and 4, depending on where the fractional epicenter\n          falls inside its enclosing 2\u00d72 grid square. The injection\n          skip is <code>d\u00b2 &lt; 0.5</code> where d is the Euclidean\n          distance from a grid cell center to the epicenter:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Epicenter (100.0, 50.0)  \u2014 integer:\n  Only cell (100, 50): d\u00b2 = 0.00  \u2192  1 cell skipped\n\nEpicenter (100.3, 50.3)  \u2014 moderate fractional:\n  Only cell (100, 50): d\u00b2 = 0.18  \u2192  1 cell skipped\n\nEpicenter (100.4, 50.7)  \u2014 offset fractional:\n  Cell (100, 51): d\u00b2 = 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.25  \u2192  skipped\n  Cell (101, 51): d\u00b2 = 0.36 + 0.09 = 0.45  \u2192  skipped\n  Cell (100, 50): d\u00b2 = 0.16 + 0.49 = 0.65  \u2192  not skipped\n  Cell (101, 50): d\u00b2 = 0.36 + 0.49 = 0.85  \u2192  not skipped\n  \u2192  2 cells skipped\n\nEpicenter (100.5, 50.5)  \u2014 half-integer:\n  All four corners at d\u00b2 = 0.50 exactly  \u2192  0 skipped\n  (strict &lt; 0.5 check passes them all through)</pre>\n        <p>\n          The Deluge Now button hits the container center, which is\n          typically at near-integer grid coordinates (1 skipped cell);\n          v0.70's <code>Math.round</code> approach caught that case.\n          Random clicks at arbitrary positions can produce the\n          offset-fractional case with 2 skipped cells; v0.70 caught\n          one but not the other.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Fix</h3>\n        <p>\n          Scan all 4 candidates in the 2\u00d72 grid around each epicenter,\n          and smooth any cell whose <code>d\u00b2 &lt; 0.5</code> matches\n          the injection's skip threshold.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">for (const ec of epicenters) {\n  const fx = Math.floor(ec.x), cx = Math.ceil(ec.x);\n  const fy = Math.floor(ec.y), cy = Math.ceil(ec.y);\n  const candidates = [[fx, fy], [cx, fy], [fx, cy], [cx, cy]];\n  for (const [ix, iy] of candidates) {\n    const dxc = ix - ec.x, dyc = iy - ec.y;\n    if (dxc*dxc + dyc*dyc &gt;= 0.5) continue;  // same check the injection uses\n    // ... smooth this cell with its 4 cardinal neighbors ...\n  }\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          No dedup needed: when <code>ec.x</code> or\n          <code>ec.y</code> is integer-valued, some candidates\n          duplicate (<code>floor === ceil</code>). The smoothing\n          operation is idempotent &mdash; replacing a cell's value\n          with the average of its 4 cardinal neighbors, then\n          repeating, produces the same value the second time because\n          the neighbors haven't changed. Redundant work is wasted,\n          not wrong.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Verification</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Test: ec at (324.4, 270.7) \u2014 fractional with 2 skipped cells\nSkipped: (324, 271) d\u00b2=0.25  AND  (325, 271) d\u00b2=0.45\n\nAfter splash (post-smoothing):\n  (324, 271) \u2014 first skipped cell:    g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (325, 271) \u2014 second skipped cell:   g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (324, 270) \u2014 not skipped:           g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (325, 270) \u2014 not skipped:           g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (323, 271) \u2014 reference neighbor:    g=0.490  d=0.010\n  \u2192 all cells match. No pinpoint.</pre>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.70 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.71. Sorry \u2014 should have caught this when I tested v0.70 by also throwing a fractional coordinate at it instead of just the integer container-center case.\n\n## What I missed\n\nThe injection skip condition is `d\u00b2 < 0.5` \u2014 Euclidean distance squared from each grid cell center to the floating-point epicenter. The number of cells that satisfy this depends entirely on where the fractional epicenter falls inside its 2\u00d72 enclosing grid square:\n\n| Epicenter type | Example | Cells skipped |\n|---|---|---|\n| Integer | (100.0, 50.0) | 1 (the cell itself) |\n| Moderate fractional | (100.3, 50.3) | 1 (closest corner) |\n| **Offset fractional** | **(100.4, 50.7)** | **2 cells** (both at y=51) |\n| Half-integer | (100.5, 50.5) | 0 (all four corners at d\u00b2=0.5 exactly, strict < fails) |\n\nThe \"Deluge Now\" button hits container center which is near-integer in grid coordinates \u2192 1 skipped cell \u2192 v0.70 caught it. Random clicks at arbitrary positions land at offset-fractional grid coordinates after `pxToGrid` conversion \u2192 2 skipped cells \u2192 v0.70's `Math.round` smoothed one and left the other as a pinpoint.\n\n## Fix\n\nScan all 4 candidates in the 2\u00d72 grid around the epicenter, smooth any with `d\u00b2 < 0.5`:\n\n```js\nconst fx = Math.floor(ec.x), cx = Math.ceil(ec.x);\nconst fy = Math.floor(ec.y), cy = Math.ceil(ec.y);\nconst candidates = [[fx, fy], [cx, fy], [fx, cy], [cx, cy]];\nfor (const [ix, iy] of candidates) {\n  const dxc = ix - ec.x, dyc = iy - ec.y;\n  if (dxc*dxc + dyc*dyc >= 0.5) continue;  // same threshold the injection uses\n  // ... smooth this cell ...\n}\n```\n\nNo dedup needed when `ec.x` or `ec.y` is integer-valued and candidates duplicate: the smoothing is idempotent (cell's value becomes neighbor average; running it again produces the same value since neighbors haven't changed).\n\n## Verification\n\n```\nTest: ec at (324.4, 270.7) \u2014 fractional, 2 skipped cells\nSkipped: (324, 271) d\u00b2=0.25  AND  (325, 271) d\u00b2=0.45\n\nAfter splash (post-smoothing):\n  (324, 271) \u2014 first skipped cell:   g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (325, 271) \u2014 second skipped cell:  g=0.490  d=0.010\n  (323, 271) \u2014 reference neighbor:   g=0.490  d=0.010\n  \u2192 all cells match. No pinpoint.\n```\n\n## Lesson (again)\n\nI tested v0.70 with one epicenter shape (the centered Deluge Now case) and shipped. The fix worked for that one case and failed for the broader class. Should have:\n\n1. Listed all the call sites that produce epicenters and what coordinates they pass\n2. Worked out the grid-skip math for each\n3. Verified the fix on each shape\n\nThis is the same pattern as the cross artifact \u2014 I'd run my test and seen what I expected to see and assumed I was done. The general lesson: when a fix targets an asymmetry caused by a fractional / boundary condition, *test the boundary cases*, not just the convenient ones.\n\nTry a few random clicks now \u2014 the pinpoint should be gone regardless of where you click.",
    "citations": []
  }
]
